issue_title: node-api: TSFN user-provided Finalize callback executed before draining queue on aborted TSFN
pr_title: worker: add postMessageToThread
combined_text:
node-api: TSFN user-provided Finalize callback executed before draining queue on aborted TSFN It could be the case that the TSFN's `context` is needed in order to "clean up" data in the user-provided `call_js` callback.

Since the TSFN's user-provided finalizer is called prior to draining the queue, the finalizer may clean/delete the context, which would then be passed to the call_js_cb as an invalid pointer.

https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/ce72fccc9d3d543eee7a49038759ccd2d8d2d33c/src/node_api.cc#L424-L431

https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/ce72fccc9d3d543eee7a49038759ccd2d8d2d33c/src/node_api.cc#L297-L302 worker: add postMessageToThread This PR is a total rethinking of #53488.

This PR adds a new API to worker_threads  called `postMessageToThread` that allow to send arbitrary messages to any worker thread just identifying it by its threadId.

The recipient thread must have a listener on the new `workerMessage` event emitted on `process` otherwise the `postMessageToThread` will throw an exception.

In order not to have any thread block, the call is `async` to use `Atomics.waitAsync`.

issue_title: DEP0169 DeprecationWarning: url.parse() when url.resolve() used (node_modules context)
pr_title: url: suppress warnings from url.format/url.resolve inside node_modules
combined_text:
DEP0169 DeprecationWarning: url.parse() when url.resolve() used (node_modules context) ### Version

24.x & 25.x - see https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/61724#issuecomment-3883242909 for details

### Platform

```text
Linux ubuntu-24.04 6.17.0-14-generic #14~24.04.1-Ubuntu SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Thu Jan 15 15:52:10 UTC 2 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux
```

### Subsystem

url

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

Run example from Legacy URL API [url.resolve(from, to)](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest/api/url.html#urlresolvefrom-to)

```js
const url = require('node:url');
url.resolve('/one/two/three', 'four');         // '/one/two/four'
url.resolve('http://example.com/', '/one');    // 'http://example.com/one'
url.resolve('http://example.com/one', '/two'); // 'http://example.com/two'
```


### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

For the above steps, it always reproduces in Node.js 25.4.0 - 25.6.0. It is a regression from Node.js 25.3.0.

If the `url.resolve()` call is embedded in an npm package, it depends how the corresponding module is called, whether or not a deprecation warning appears. See the additional information section below for examples.

### What is the expected behavior? Why is that the expected behavior?

The example with only `url.resolve()` should run without producing a deprecation warning.

The history section of [url.resolve(from, to)](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest/api/url.html#urlresolvefrom-to) states that the deprecation was revoked in v15.13.0, v14.17.0 and there is no entry for any higher version.

[DEP0169](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest/api/deprecations.html#DEP0169) lists [url.parse()](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest/api/url.html#urlparseurlstring-parsequerystring-slashesdenotehost) explicitly. It does not include any direct reference to `url.resolve()`.

### What do you see instead?

```text
$ node url-resolve.js
(node:5802) [DEP0169] DeprecationWarning: `url.parse()` behavior is not standardized and prone to errors that have security implications. Use the WHATWG URL API instead. CVEs are not issued for `url.parse()` vulnerabilities.
(Use `node --trace-deprecation ...` to show where the warning was created)
```


### Additional information

I originally noticed this with Cypress, on Ubuntu 24.04.3 LTS, Node.js 25.6.0, with steps to reproduce:

```shell
cd $(mktemp -d)
npm install cypress
npx cypress install --force
```

where the call is from `/node_modules/cypress/dist/tasks/download.js`.

The following steps however do **NOT** cause any deprecation warning, even though download is also run:

```shell
rm -rf ~/.cache/Cypress
cd $(mktemp -d)
npm install cypress
```
 url: suppress warnings from url.format/url.resolve inside node_modules Fixes: #61724


### Additional information

I use nvm to switch node versions & stay up to date module: unflag --experimental-require-module This unflags --experimental-require-module so require(esm) can be used without the flag. For now, when require() actually encounters an ESM for the first time in a process, it will still emit an experimental warning. To opt out of the feature, --no-experimental-require-module can be used.

There are some tests specifically testing ERR_REQUIRE_ESM. Some of them are repurposed to test --no-experimental-require-module. Some of them are modified to just expect loading require(esm) to work, when it's appropriate.

This is expected to go out in 23 and get some testing before being backported to older LTS.

See https://github.com/nodejs/node/pull/55085#issuecomment-2371022961 for a summary of the impact of this on loading the high-impact npm packages.

For more background on the motivation of `require(esm)` see https://github.com/nodejs/node/pull/51977 - TL;DR: it helps accelerating ESM adoption in the ecosystem as package authors can start shipping native ESM with less breakage to their CJS users; it also helps frameworks and tools that take plugins to support native ESM in user/plugin code whilst they are still navigating their own migration to ESM.

Note to releasers: in the release announcements we should emphasize the implications this has on top-level await is limited to require(). In entry points or modules that are only ever imported, top-level await works fine as before. Only when one tries to use the synchronous require() to evaluate a module that awaits at module loading time (top-level), it obviously would not work, not that it ever worked before require() supports ESM, just that it's now the only significant remaining exception for require(esm).

Refs: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/52697

issue_title: Increase min_semi_space_size when possible to make v8 default heap space sizes less catastrophic for containers and memory-constrained environments
pr_title: fs: pass correct path to `DirentFromStats` during `glob`
combined_text:
Increase min_semi_space_size when possible to make v8 default heap space sizes less catastrophic for containers and memory-constrained environments ### What is the problem this feature will solve?

Since upgrading from Node 20 to 24, I've been struggling to figure out why our production app memory usage slowly increases, but then the app starts becoming responsive instead of running OOM.

@mcollina I just ran into [your article on Node.js heap tuning](https://blog.platformatic.dev/optimizing-nodejs-performance-v8-memory-management-and-gc-tuning) which mentions the following, and explains a lot about what I've been seeing.  But ARGH why wasn't this in the [release notes for Node 22](https://nodejs.org/en/blog/announcements/v22-release-announce)???  This was crucial to warn people about so they don't get shafted!

> An essential nuance in V8's memory management emerged around the Node.js v22 release cycle concerning how the default size for the New Space semi-spaces is determined. Unlike some earlier versions with more static defaults, newer V8 versions incorporate heuristics that attempt to set this default size dynamically, often based on the total amount of memory perceived as available to the Node.js process when it starts.  This is highly relevant for applications deployed in containers (like Docker on Kubernetes) or serverless platforms (like AWS Lambda or Google Cloud Functions) where memory limits are often set relatively low (e.g., 512MB, 1GB, 2GB). In such scenarios, V8's dynamic calculation might result in an unexpectedly small default --max-semi-space-size, sometimes as low as 1 MB or 8 MB.

> ... Therefore, for applications running on Node.js v22 or later within memory-limited contexts, relying solely on the default V8 settings for semi-space size is generally discouraged. Developers should strongly consider profiling their application and explicitly setting the --max-semi-space-size flag

### What is the feature you are proposing to solve the problem?

> In such scenarios, V8's dynamic calculation might result in an unexpectedly small default `--max-semi-space-size`, sometimes as low as 1 MB or 8 MB.

Prevent this from happening by passing a default [`min_semi_space_size`](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/v24.2.0/deps/v8/src/flags/flag-definitions.h#L2052C15-L2052C34) to V8 with a high enough value that the auto semi space size doesn't go below what older versions of Node used, unless the user overrides it or other constraints given by the user/detected available memory are so low that would be unreasonable.

### What alternatives have you considered?

This is making me question whether I can trust Node.js maintenance to be predictable going forward. fs: pass correct path to `DirentFromStats` during `glob` Fixes #55060

Passes the dirname as parentPath, rather than the full file path.

issue_title: Agents: Reuse VS Code auth
pr_title: Share secrets between Code and Agents app via macOS Keychain
combined_text:
Agents: Reuse VS Code auth <!-- ⚠️⚠️ Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ⚠️⚠️ -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->
Reusing the same auth from VS Code in Agents (so that users don't have to re-sign in) would help make getting started with the Agents app even smoother. Share secrets between Code and Agents app via macOS Keychain Fixes #308028

Add a shared keychain service that stores secrets directly in the macOS Keychain, allowing Code and its embedded Agents app to share auth tokens without re-authentication.

## Architecture

- `ISharedKeychainService` (common interface) with `ISharedKeychainMainService` running in the Electron main process, exposed to renderer via IPC (`registerMainProcessRemoteService`)
- `SharedKeychainMainService` wraps the `@vscode/macos-keychain` native addon
- `NativeSecretStorageService` now writes to both the shared keychain and the legacy safeStorage+SQLite pipeline (for rollback safety)
- On read, shared keychain is tried first, falling back to legacy
- Shared keychain is only used when `type === 'persisted'` (not in-memory)

## Product configuration

- `darwinSharedKeychainServiceName` — per-flavor keychain service name for data isolation between Stable/Insiders/Exploration (set in product.json)
- Access group is auto-detected from the process's `keychain-access-groups` entitlement by the native addon at module load time (via `SecTaskCopyValueForEntitlement`)

## Base class refactoring

`BaseSecretStorageService` now exposes protected `_doGet`/`_doSet`/`_doDelete`/`_doGetKeys` methods that perform the actual safeStorage+SQLite operations without going through the sequencer, so subclasses can call them from within their own sequencer-queued tasks without deadlocking.

## Native addon

`@vscode/macos-keychain` — see https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-macos-keychain
- PR #1: Initial addon (merged)
- PR #2: Auto-detect access group from entitlements (open)

## TODO

- [x] Migration logic (copy existing secrets from legacy pipeline to shared keychain on startup)
- [x] Remove `debugger` statements
- [x] Verify compilation
- [ ] Integration test with real auth tokens

Enable terminal commands to run freely in `Autopilot` and `Bypass Approvals` modes Reverts https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/300824 which fixed eval issues by returning early from the tool if a denied command was attempted. This caused issues like https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/303174, where commands were disallowed vs prompted for. Now, we will skip the `auto approval` feature/system when a user is in `Autopilot` or `Bypass Approvals` mode so terminal commands run freely. Also specifies "terminal commands" in `Autopilot` dialog (`Bypass Approvals` already had this). Address #303174 in main. This will be a candidate.

issue_title: Terminal commands should be auto approved when invoked from `ask_questions` tool
pr_title: Skip redundant `send_to_terminal` confirmation when replying to `askQuestions` carousel
combined_text:
Terminal commands should be auto approved when invoked from `ask_questions` tool Since the user already confirmed their answers, we should not prompt, esp about sending `Enter`.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1089c44c-9ada-40cb-89c1-2f40b8cf10a8 Skip redundant `send_to_terminal` confirmation when replying to `askQuestions` carousel Addresses #309589 in main
Addresses https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/309608 in main

When an interactive command (e.g. `npm init`) waits for input in default approvals mode, the agent calls `askQuestions` to collect user answers, then sends each answer via `send_to_terminal`. Previously, a `send_to_terminal` call could show a confirmation dialog, even though the user just explicitly provided that text through the askQuestions UI.

This skips the confirmation when we can correlate the `send_to_terminal` text to a recently answered question carousel for the same terminal.

### Changes

**Confirmation skip** — `_getQuestionContextForTerminal` already returns the question text for the "(replying to: …)" display. A non-`undefined` result now also signals that confirmation is unnecessary. `forceConfirmationReason` still takes precedence, and autopilot mode behavior is unchanged.

**Exact answer matching** — Previously, `_getQuestionContextForTerminal` had shortcuts that could return a question without verifying the command matched the answer (single-question shortcut, multi-question fallback). This meant any `send_to_terminal` call could bypass confirmation whenever a recent carousel existed for that terminal. Now the method only returns a question when the command exactly matches the answer at the correct position.

**Positional matching** — When all answers are identical (e.g. pressing Enter to accept every default in `npm init`), answer-value matching always returned the first question. The method now counts prior `send_to_terminal` tool invocations after the carousel to determine the positional index, so the progress message correctly shows "Package name?" → "version?" → "description?" instead of repeating "Package name?" for every prompt.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9edfa037-06d5-4f0f-8773-f19ce232c784

issue_title: `send_to_terminal` progress message repeats first question when accepting all defaults in interactive commands
pr_title: Skip redundant `send_to_terminal` confirmation when replying to `askQuestions` carousel
combined_text:
`send_to_terminal` progress message repeats first question when accepting all defaults in interactive commands ## Bug

When an interactive command like `npm init` is run and the user accepts all defaults via the `askQuestions` carousel, every `send_to_terminal` progress message displays the first question's text (e.g. "Package name?") instead of the correct question for each prompt.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open a chat session in default approvals mode
2. Ask the agent to run `npm init` in a terminal
3. The agent detects the interactive prompts and shows an `askQuestions` carousel with multiple questions (package name, version, description, entry point, etc.)
4. Accept all defaults by leaving every field empty and submitting the carousel
5. The agent sends each default answer via `send_to_terminal` (pressing Enter for each prompt)

## Expected Behavior

Each `send_to_terminal` progress message should show the corresponding question:

- Pressed `Enter` in terminal (replying to: Package name?)
- Pressed `Enter` in terminal (replying to: version?)
- Pressed `Enter` in terminal (replying to: description?)
- …

## Actual Behavior

Every progress message repeats the first question:

- Pressed `Enter` in terminal (replying to: Package name? (default: @xterm/xterm))
- Pressed `Enter` in terminal (replying to: Package name? (default: @xterm/xterm))
- Pressed `Enter` in terminal (replying to: Package name? (default: @xterm/xterm))
- …

## Cause

`_getQuestionContextForTerminal` matches the sent command text against carousel answer values to find the corresponding question. When all answers are identical (empty string for defaults), the loop iterates questions in order and always matches the first one.

## Fix

Use positional matching: count prior `send_to_terminal` tool invocations after the carousel in the response parts to determine the question index, rather than relying solely on answer-value matching.
 Skip redundant `send_to_terminal` confirmation when replying to `askQuestions` carousel Addresses #309589 in main
Addresses https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/309608 in main

When an interactive command (e.g. `npm init`) waits for input in default approvals mode, the agent calls `askQuestions` to collect user answers, then sends each answer via `send_to_terminal`. Previously, a `send_to_terminal` call could show a confirmation dialog, even though the user just explicitly provided that text through the askQuestions UI.

This skips the confirmation when we can correlate the `send_to_terminal` text to a recently answered question carousel for the same terminal.

### Changes

**Confirmation skip** — `_getQuestionContextForTerminal` already returns the question text for the "(replying to: …)" display. A non-`undefined` result now also signals that confirmation is unnecessary. `forceConfirmationReason` still takes precedence, and autopilot mode behavior is unchanged.

**Exact answer matching** — Previously, `_getQuestionContextForTerminal` had shortcuts that could return a question without verifying the command matched the answer (single-question shortcut, multi-question fallback). This meant any `send_to_terminal` call could bypass confirmation whenever a recent carousel existed for that terminal. Now the method only returns a question when the command exactly matches the answer at the correct position.

**Positional matching** — When all answers are identical (e.g. pressing Enter to accept every default in `npm init`), answer-value matching always returned the first question. The method now counts prior `send_to_terminal` tool invocations after the carousel to determine the positional index, so the progress message correctly shows "Package name?" → "version?" → "description?" instead of repeating "Package name?" for every prompt.

Deleting forked session deleted my worktree https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/bc669713-7077-41ed-ac68-b54748eac5a4


The first time I tried this, it only warned about deleting the worktree, it didn't actually delete anything. Then I tried it a second time, and it actually not only deleted the worktree, my whole original session is completely gone and doesn't show up anywhere in the 'done' list that I can see Fix deleting forked session showing wrong confirmation dialog When clicking X on a sub-session tab, `deleteChat` checks `chatIds.length <= 1` before finding the specific chat by URI. If the grouping cache doesn't include the forked chat (e.g. due to stale `sessionParentId` metadata), `_getChatIdsInGroup` returns only the main chat. The `chatIds.length <= 1` check then falls through to `deleteSession`, which shows "delete this session?" and deletes the entire session including the worktree.

**Fix:** Find the specific chat by URI first, then check if it's the last chat. Only fall through to `deleteSession` if the chat IS found AND it's the last one. If the chat isn't in the current grouping result, return early instead of accidentally deleting the whole session.

Fixes #313148

issue_title: After Plan mode, agent does not start implementation
pr_title: On agent mode change, ignore stateful marker
combined_text: After Plan mode, agent does not start implementation Type: Bug I asked GPT 5.4 xhigh to create a plan, after the plan is done and saved in session memory, i press Start implementation, Chat response "Implementation is blocked by the current planning-only mode. I cannot edit files or start code changes from this agent state." then i simply type in chat, i switched to agent mode, start imeplementation and the agent is starting to do the job, i lost a few premium requests because of this issue. VS Code version: Code 1.117.0 (10c8e557c8b9f9ed0a87f61f1c9a44bde731c409, 2026-04-21T16:12:14-07:00) OS version: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26100 Modes: On agent mode change, ignore stateful marker Fixes #312554 ## Problem #312554 reports that after a user finishes a plan and presses **Start implementation**, Copilot can still answer: > Implementation is blocked by the current planning-only mode. I cannot edit files or start code changes from this agent state. Plan mode itself should remain planning-only/read-only. The bug is that after the UI/request mode changes out of Plan mode, Responses API stateful context can still continue from a marker that was produced under the previous mode. Responses API requests may reuse a stateful marker / `previous_response_id` so we do not resend the full conversation every time. That is normally correct, but those markers are tied to the stateful chain that produced them, including the active mode instructions. If a request switches modes, for example **Agent -> Plan** or **Plan -> Agent / Start implementation**, reusing or slicing history from the old marker can cause the model to stay anchored to the old mode. In the issue scenario, the first implementation request after Plan mode could still be interpreted as part of the planning-only chain, so the model refused to edit. ## Why `modeChanged` is needed The default request handler is where we can compare VS Code chat mode instructions from one turn to the next. The Responses API request builder is where we decide whether it is safe to reuse a stateful marker / `previous_response_id`. This branch adds a `modeChanged` signal between those layers: - when mode instructions changed, the first Responses API request in the new mode does **not** reuse the previous stateful marker - that request establishes a fresh stateful chain for the new mode - follow-up requests in the same turn then reuse the newly established marker normally That means: - **Agent -> Plan**: the first Plan request does not continue from the old implementation chain - Plan-mode follow-ups after tool calls can continue from the new Plan marker - **Plan -> Agent / Start implementation**: the first implementation request does not continue from the planning-only chain - implementation follow-ups after tool calls can continue from the new implementation marker This fixes #312554 because **Start implementation** is treated as a real mode transition instead of as another request on the Plan-mode Responses API chain. ## What changed in this branch ### Preserve mode instructions on conversation turns - `Turn.fromRequest` now stores `request.modeInstructions2` on the `Turn`. - Turns reconstructed from VS Code chat history also preserve `modeInstructions2`. - `EditCodeIntent` preserves `latestTurn.modeInstructions` when it rebuilds a conversation after wrapping codebase tool-call results. This gives later request handling enough information to compare the previous turn's mode instructions with the current request's mode instructions. ### Detect mode transitions in the default intent request path - `DefaultToolCallingLoop.didModeChangeSincePreviousRequest()` compares the previous turn's mode instructions with the current request's mode instructions. - The comparison includes URI, name, content, built-in flag, and normalized metadata so equivalent metadata with different key ordering is treated as unchanged. - The result is sent to the endpoint as `modeChanged` only for Responses API endpoints. - Non-Responses endpoints keep their existing behavior. ### Apply mode changes in Responses API request construction - `IMakeChatRequestOptions` now includes `modeChanged?: boolean`. - `createResponsesRequestBody` passes `modeChanged` into `rawMessagesToResponseAPI`. - When `modeChanged` is true, `rawMessagesToResponseAPI` clears both: - `previous_response_id` - the local stateful-marker index used for history slicing This applies to both HTTP and websocket Responses API requests, preventing the first request in a new mode from being sliced after, or resumed from, a stale marker from the old mode. ### Preserve the new marker for same-turn follow-ups - `ToolCallingLoop` exposes the current tool-call rounds to subclasses. - Once the current turn has produced a fresh Responses API stateful marker, `didModeChangeSincePreviousRequest()` returns false for later requests in that same turn. This is important for websocket-enabled Responses API models. After the first mode-switched request succeeds, tool-call follow-ups should continue from the newly established mode-specific websocket marker instead of repeatedly invalidating marker reuse for the whole turn. ### Clarify Plan-mode tool restrictions - Added coverage that the default Plan agent exposes only the default read tools plus `agent` and `vscode/askQuestions`. - The test explicitly checks that Plan mode does not expose mutation tools like `edit`, `createFile`, or `apply_patch`. That keeps the Plan-mode guardrail explicit while the `modeChanged` handling fixes the transition into and out of Plan mode. ## Tests Added/updated coverage for: - detecting changed mode instructions on Responses API requests - preserving default marker behavior when mode instructions are unchanged - not reusing a websocket stateful marker when `modeChanged` is true - not reusing an HTTP stateful marker when `modeChanged` is true - reusing the newly established websocket marker on follow-up requests after switching into Plan mode - treating **Agent -> Plan** and **Plan -> Agent / Start implementation** websocket requests as separate mode changes - Plan mode's default tool set staying read-only, with only `agent` and `vscode/askQuestions` added

issue_title: Copilot chat OTel tools are missing parameters
pr_title: OTel: include tool parameters and emit tool definitions on child chat spans
combined_text:
Copilot chat OTel tools are missing parameters <!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates -->
<!-- Please attach logs to help us diagnose your issue -->

```
Version: 1.111.0-insider (user setup)
Commit: de052c15eaefaceaa5b673081e2834f1cdf193db
Date: 2026-03-05T17:35:21-08:00
Electron: 39.6.0
ElectronBuildId: 13330601
Chromium: 142.0.7444.265
Node.js: 22.22.0
V8: 14.2.231.22-electron.0
OS: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200
```

Steps to Reproduce:

1. Enable OpenTelemetry and point it at the standalone Aspire dashboard
2. Use different models (i.e. Opus) to perform operations
3. View traces in Aspire dashboard
4. See some spans with tool definitions.
5. Open GenAI visualizer and view tools. Only name and description are available. Parameters are missing. This is because parameters from the tool's inputSchema aren't set: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-copilot-chat/blob/990402108a0268f786ea8988d685354e8bb96729/src/extension/intents/node/toolCallingLoop.ts#L729

https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/semconv/registry/attributes/gen-ai/#gen-ai-tool-definitions

cc @zhichli
 OTel: include tool parameters and emit tool definitions on child chat spans Fixes #300318
Fixes #299934

## What

OTel `gen_ai.tool.definitions` for chat agent traces was missing two things:

1. **`parameters` (tool input schema)** — only `{ type, name, description }` was emitted, so trace viewers like the Aspire GenAI visualizer rendered tools with no parameter table.
2. **Tool definitions on child `chat {model}` spans** — only the root `invoke_agent` span carried the catalog, so a chat span on its own (e.g. when filtered/expanded in a viewer) appeared without tools even though the model was clearly invoking them.

## Changes

- `toolCallingLoop.ts`: agent root span attribute and `tools_available` event now include `parameters: tool.inputSchema`.
- `chatMLFetcher.ts`: each `chat {model}` child span sets `gen_ai.tool.definitions` from `requestBody.tools` (truncated for OTel attribute limits).
- BYOK `anthropicProvider.ts` / `geminiNativeProvider.ts`: same on their chat spans (gated by `captureContent`).
- `messageFormatters.ts` `toToolDefinitions` extended to normalize all tool variants we emit:
  - OpenAI Chat Completions `{ type: 'function', function: { name, description, parameters } }`
  - OpenAI Responses API `{ type: 'function', name, description, parameters }`
  - Anthropic Messages `{ name, description, input_schema }`
  - VS Code `{ name, description, inputSchema }`
- New unit tests for the additional shapes.

## Verification

End-to-end verified against a local Aspire dashboard with claude-haiku-4.5 (trace `9bb5af1f6353dad136b2879c999b2598`):

| Check | Span | Result |
|---|---|---|
| `parameters` rendered in tool table | `invoke_agent GitHub Copilot Chat` | ✅ `todoList * array<object>` shown |
| Tools tab populated on child span | `chat claude-haiku-4.5` | ✅ 63 definitions |
| `parameters` on child span too | `chat claude-haiku-4.5` | ✅ same parameter table |

Unit tests: 45/45 pass (`messageFormatters.spec.ts`, `genAiEvents.spec.ts`).
Typecheck: clean across `extensions/copilot`.

cc @JamesNK

issue_title: Agents App: Session List Visual Improvements
pr_title: sessions: add pinned sidebar section
combined_text: Agents App: Session List Visual Improvements # Session List Visual Improvements ## Overview The session list needs better visual hierarchy, improved readability (especially in light theme), and more engaging feedback for in-progress sessions. **Related Files:** - `src/vs/sessions/contrib/sessions/browser/views/sessionsList.ts` - `src/vs/sessions/contrib/sessions/browser/media/sessionsList.css` ## Issues & Opportunities ### 1. Color Contrast (Light Theme Priority) **Problem:** Text contrast between session titles and timestamps is too low, making the list difficult to scan. **Potential Solutions:** - [ ] Increase contrast ratio for secondary text (timestamps) in light theme - [ ] Audit all text colors against WCAG AA standards (4.5:1 for normal text) - [ ] Ensure session titles stand out as the primary element - [ ] Consider subtle weight differences (medium vs. regular) to establish hierarchy **Current Implementation:** ```typescript // From sessionsList.ts private static readonly ITEM_HEIGHT = 54; ``` Each item has title + metadata (time), but contrast may need adjustment. ### 2. Visual Noise from Separator Dots **Problem:** Dots between session type icon and text create visual clutter. **Potential Solutions:** - [ ] Remove separator dots entirely - [ ] Test spacing alone to separate icon from text - [ ] Consider alternative subtle separators if needed (light vertical line, margin only, etc.) - [ ] Validate with various session types and icon combinations ### 3. In-Progress Session Indicator Animation **Problem:** Current in-progress icon feels static for a "pro-sumer" product; missed opportunity for engaging micro-interaction. **Potential Solutions:** - [ ] Add subtle animation/motion to in-progress indicator - [ ] Options to explore: - Gentle pulsing (fade in/out) - Subtle rotation or shimmer - Breathing animation (scale or opacity) - Gradient shift - [ ] Ensure animation is: - Subtle enough not to distract - Respectful of motion preferences (`prefers-reduced-motion`) - Performance-friendly (CSS animations, not JS) ### 4. Overall List Scannability **Problem:** Items may not differentiate enough visually to enable quick scanning. **Potential Solutions:** - [ ] Ensure adequate vertical spacing between items - [ ] Consider hover states that feel responsive and polished - [ ] Add subtle dividers or background treatments for separation if needed ## Success Criteria - [ ] Session list is easy to scan at a glance - [ ] Color contrast meets or exceeds WCAG AA standards - [ ] Visual hierarchy clearly distinguishes titles from metadata - [ ] In-progress sessions feel dynamic without being distracting - [ ] Overall aesthetic feels modern and "pro-sumer" quality ## Design Notes - We can be slightly more expressive than traditional developer tools - Motion should enhance the experience, not distract from it - Always respect user accessibility preferences (reduced motion, high contrast) ## Implementation Considerations ```css /* Example animation approach for in-progress icon */ @keyframes session-in-progress { 0%, 100% { opacity: 1; } 50% { opacity: 0.6; } } .session-in-progress-icon { animation: session-in-progress 2s ease-in-out infinite; } @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { .session-in-progress-icon { animation: none; } } ``` sessions: add pinned sidebar section ## Summary Related to https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/310018 - render pinned chats under a dedicated `Pinned` section header in the sessions sidebar - reuse the existing section-header presentation so the label stays uppercase like workspace and folder headers - keep the pinned section toolbar limited to `Mark All as Done`, without workspace-specific actions like `New Session` - add grouping tests and update the sessions layout spec ## Notes - compile-check and hygiene passed in this environment - the targeted Electron-backed unit test bootstrap failed in this CLI environment before reaching the sessions code (`app.setPath` on an undefined Electron app)

*This issue was automatically created from the VS Code Errors Dashboard* Don't throw in setRequestTools when agent is gone Fixes #312803

The `autorun` in `chatServiceImpl` that calls `setRequestTools` is per-request and can fire after the target agent has been unregistered (e.g., `setup.vscode` is disposed when Copilot setup completes while a request is still in-flight). Throwing here surfaces as an unhandled error from inside the autorun.

Match the existing pattern used by `setYieldRequested` and `getFollowups` in the same file and silently return when the agent is no longer registered.

There's only one caller of `setRequestTools` (`chatServiceImpl.ts:1266`) and it always passes the id of an agent it just successfully invoked, so the throw was never catching real misuse — only this lifecycle race.

issue_title: Agent customizations list item should focus search input on Enter/Space
pr_title: Focus search input on section selection for keyboard parity
combined_text:
Agent customizations list item should focus search input on Enter/Space ## Issue

In the agent customizations list, activating a list item via keyboard (pressing `Enter` or `Space`) does not move focus to the search input. This breaks keyboard parity with the mouse-click behavior and forces keyboard / screen reader users to manually traverse to the search input after selecting an item.

## Expected

Pressing `Enter` or `Space` on a focused agent customizations list item should:
- Perform the item's primary action, AND
- Move focus to the search input so the user can immediately filter/search without an extra `Tab` traversal.

## Actual

Pressing `Enter` or `Space` on the list item does not move focus to the search input. Focus remains on the list, requiring additional keyboard navigation to reach the search input.

## Impact

- Keyboard-only users and screen reader users have a degraded flow compared to mouse users.
- Inconsistent activation behavior across `Enter`/`Space` versus click is a WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard) parity concern.

## Repro

1. Open the agent customizations view.
2. Tab into the list and focus a customization item.
3. Press `Enter` (or `Space`).
4. Observe: focus does not land on the search input.
 Focus search input on section selection for keyboard parity When a section is selected via keyboard (Enter/Space) in the agent customizations sidebar, focus now moves to the search input of the active content widget. This matches the mouse-click behavior and fixes a WCAG 2.1.1 keyboard parity concern.

Fixes #312817

issue_title: kubelet TopologyManager Stalls on Large NUMA Systems (e.g. Nvidia GB200 Compute Tray with 34 NUMA nodes)
pr_title: devicemanager: constrain topology hints to device NUMA nodes
combined_text:
kubelet TopologyManager Stalls on Large NUMA Systems (e.g. Nvidia GB200 Compute Tray with 34 NUMA nodes) ### What happened?

When scheduling a pod (e.g. KubeVirt virt-launcher pod) that requests a PCI device on a very large NUMA system, kubelet’s TopologyManager never finishes computing topology hints and the pod is effectively stuck in admission:

- The pod is scheduled to the node.
- kubelet never reaches the device Allocate call for the requested resource.
- From a higher-level consumer (KubeVirt in our case), the virt-launcher pod stays in Pending and the VM remains in Starting with Ready = False.
- kubelet is responsive, but many goroutines are stuck in topology hint generation.

In our environment this manifests as:

- KubeVirt VM with a hostDevice (GPU, InfiniBand or NVMe) never transitions to Running, even though the node has the capacity and the device is free.
- Disabling TopologyManager or constraining the NUMA fan-out immediately lets the VM start

### What did you expect to happen?

kubelet should compute topology hints in bounded time, even on systems with many NUMA nodes (in a case GB200 with 34 NUMA nodes where only 2 NUMA nodes have CPUs, the rest are for HBM/NVLink), and call device-plugin Allocate so that pods/VMs using PCI host devices can start.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

## High-level repro:
1. Use a host with a large NUMA topology  
    Example: Nvidia GB200-class system with 34 NUMA nodes visible to the OS.
 
2. Run Kubernetes with kubelet configured roughly as follows:
```yaml
kubeReserved:
  cpu: "2"
  memory: 4Gi
  ephemeral-storage: 20Gi
systemReserved:
  cpu: "2"
  memory: 8Gi
  ephemeral-storage: 20Gi
topologyManagerPolicy: best-effort
topologyManagerScope: pod
topologyManagerPolicyOptions:
  prefer-closest-numa-nodes: "true"
  max-allowable-numa-nodes: "34"
cpuManagerPolicy: static
reservedSystemCPUs: "0-3,72-75"
cpuManagerPolicyOptions:
  distribute-cpus-across-numa: "false"
```
3. Deploy a device plugin that:
- Advertises a PCI resource (e.g. GPU, InfiniBand or NVMe) as a kubelet device resource.
- Exposes the device with topology information referencing many NUMA nodes (up to 34 in our case).

4. Create a Pod
Requests one unit of that device resource.
Uses Guaranteed QoS (requests == limits for CPU/memory) so that TopologyManager participates fully.
 
5. Observe
- The pod is scheduled to the node.
- kubelet never calls Allocate on the device plugin for that resource.
- The pod stays in Pending indefinitely.
- KubeVirt-specific repro (how we hit it)
- Instead of a raw pod, create a KubeVirt VirtualMachineInstance that uses hostDevices:
```yaml
devices:
   hostDevices:
    - deviceName: nvidia.com/GB100_HGX_GB200
      name: gpu1
```
The pod (if using KubeVirt, it is virt-launcher pod for a VM) is scheduled to the large NUMA node but stays Pending forever. The pod stuck at Pending, (if using KubeVirt, the VM status remains Starting) and never reaches Ready, despite sufficient resources.


### Anything else we need to know?

## Root cause analysis

- The kubelet TopologyManager asks each hint provider (CPU, memory, device) to compute topology hints over the set of NUMA nodes that the kubelet knows about.
- For the device plugin case, generateDeviceTopologyHints ends up calling bitmask.IterateBitMasks across a bitmask spanning all NUMA IDs known to the machine, not just those relevant to the requested device.
- With 34 NUMA nodes this becomes effectively an exponential search (O(2^n)) over the 34-bit mask.
- On a GB200 host with 34 NUMA nodes and a device plugin that advertises per-node topology across those nodes, generateDeviceTopologyHints spends unbounded time iterating combinations that are not actually schedulable for the requested device.
- While TopologyManager is doing this, kubelet never reaches the device Allocate call, so pods/VMs that need that device are permanently stuck in admission.

## Evidence observed

- Goroutine dumps from kubelet show many workers blocked under TopologyManager/device-manager paths, repeatedly calling IterateBitMasks on a large bitmask.
- No calls to Allocate are observed for the affected device resource while the system is in this hung state.
- If we disable TopologyManager, then virt-launcher pods immediately proceed and the KubeVirt VM starts successfully.

### Kubernetes version

```console
$ kubectl version
Client Version: v1.34.2
Kustomize Version: v5.7.1
Server Version: v1.34.2
```

### Cloud provider

Bare metal, GB200 compute tray

### OS version

```console
# On Linux:
$ cat /etc/os-release
PRETTY_NAME="Ubuntu 24.04.3 LTS"
NAME="Ubuntu"
VERSION_ID="24.04"
VERSION="24.04.3 LTS (Noble Numbat)"
VERSION_CODENAME=noble
ID=ubuntu
ID_LIKE=debian
HOME_URL="https://www.ubuntu.com/"
SUPPORT_URL="https://help.ubuntu.com/"
BUG_REPORT_URL="https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/"
PRIVACY_POLICY_URL="https://www.ubuntu.com/legal/terms-and-policies/privacy-policy"
UBUNTU_CODENAME=noble
LOGO=ubuntu-logo

$ uname -a
Linux GB200-node-4 6.14.0-1013-nvidia-64k #13-Ubuntu SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Wed Oct 29 09:44:30 UTC 2025 aarch64 aarch64 aarch64 GNU/Linux
```


### Install tools

kubeadm
```
kubeadm version: &version.Info{Major:"1", Minor:"34", EmulationMajor:"", EmulationMinor:"", MinCompatibilityMajor:"", MinCompatibilityMinor:"", GitVersion:"v1.34.2", GitCommit:"8cc511e399b929453cd98ae65b419c3cc227ec79", GitTreeState:"clean", BuildDate:"2025-11-11T19:08:36Z", GoVersion:"go1.24.9", Compiler:"gc", Platform:"linux/arm64"}
```

### Related plugins (CNI, CSI, ...) and versions (if applicable)

<details>

</details>
 devicemanager: constrain topology hints to device NUMA nodes ## What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

## What this PR does / why we need it

On systems with many NUMA nodes (e.g. NVIDIA GB200 compute trays with 36 OS-visible NUMA nodes, only 2 CPU NUMA nodes), the device manager's `generateDeviceTopologyHints` calls `bitmask.IterateBitMasks` over **all** kubelet-known NUMA nodes. Since `IterateBitMasks` enumerates every non-empty subset, the cost is O(2^n) — with n=34 that is ~17 billion iterations. This causes kubelet to stall indefinitely during topology hint computation and the pod never reaches `Allocate`.

### Root cause
The iteration space is derived from `m.numaNodes`, which includes every NUMA node the OS exposes. On GB200 the majority of those nodes carry only GPU HBM (Coherent Device Memory) and host zero devices for any given
device-plugin resource. Including them inflates n without contributing useful hints.

This PR constrains the bitmask iteration to only the NUMA nodes that actually host available or reusable devices for the requested resource, reducing the search space from 2^n (machine-wide) to 2^k (device-relevant), where k is typically 1–2 on GB200 systems.

### Fix

- Introduce `deviceNUMANodes(resource)`, which collects the NUMA node IDs from **all registered devices** (`m.allDevices[resource]`) regardless of allocation state. `generateDeviceTopologyHints` now iterates only over
those nodes, reducing the search space from 2^n (machine-wide) to 2^k (device-relevant), where k is typically 1–2 on GB200 systems.

- Using `allDevices` (rather than `available ∪ reusable`) ensures that `minAffinitySize` and `Preferred` flags are computed identically to the original code — device-less NUMA nodes contribute zero to `devicesInMask`
and can never reduce `minAffinitySize`, so any mask containing them is always non-preferred. Removing them from iteration is therefore **behavior-preserving** for all existing configurations.

### Changes

1. **`deviceNUMANodes(resource)`** — new helper that returns the sorted list of NUMA node IDs hosting at least one device for the given resource. Falls back to `m.numaNodes` when no topology info exists.

2. **`generateDeviceTopologyHints()`** — now calls `IterateBitMasks(deviceNUMANodes, …)` instead of `IterateBitMasks(m.numaNodes, …)`. No synthetic fallback hint is needed because the iteration already covers every NUMA node that could satisfy a request.

3. **Unit tests** :     
   - `TestDeviceNUMANodes`: validates NUMA node collection from `allDevices` and fallback behavior.
   - `TestGenerateDeviceTopologyHintsFiltersNUMANodes`: verifies that device-less NUMA nodes are excluded from hints, including a 34-device-less-node scenario.
   - `TestFilteredDeviceHintsMergeWithOtherProviders`: confirms that filtered device hints merge correctly with CPU/memory providers through the TopologyManager.

## Which issue(s) this PR fixes

Fixes https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/135541

## How has this been tested?

- All existing `TestGetTopologyHints`, `TestGetPodTopologyHints`, and `TestTopologyAlignedAllocation` tests pass unchanged.
- `TestDeviceNUMANodes` tests validate NUMA filtering and fallback.
- `TestGenerateDeviceTopologyHintsFiltersNUMANodes ` validates hint filtering including a 34-device-less-node scenario.
- `TestFilteredDeviceHintsMergeWithOtherProviders` validates cross-provider hint merging through TopologyManager.
- Verified with `hack/verify-golangci-lint.sh pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/...`: 0 lint issues.

### Validation on NVIDIA GB200 bare-metal node

Tested on a GB200 compute tray (36 NUMA nodes) with the following kubelet configuration:
​
```yaml
cpuManagerPolicyOptions:
  distribute-cpus-across-numa: "true"
topologyManagerPolicy: best-effort
topologyManagerScope: pod
topologyManagerPolicyOptions:
  prefer-closest-numa-nodes: "true"
  max-allowable-numa-nodes: "36"
```

Created a KubeVirt VMI with `dedicatedCpuPlacement`, 120 vCPUs, 4 Blackwell GPU passthrough devices, and EGM or hugepages. With this fix applied, the VMI reaches `Running` state and `Ready=True`:
​
```
$ kubectl get vmi -owide
NAME                         AGE   PHASE     IP            NODENAME     READY   LIVE-MIGRATABLE   PAUSED
test-120cpu-numa-4gpu-vm   29m   Running   10.244.3.17   computetray-w8   True    False
```

Without this fix, the same virt-launcher pod would remain stuck in `Pending` indefinitely due to the topology hint computation stall described in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/135541.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

release-note:
```
Fixed a kubelet device manager bug where topology hint computation enumerated O(2^n) NUMA node combinations using all machine NUMA nodes. On systems with many NUMA nodes that carry no devices (e.g. NVIDIA GB200 with 36 NUMA nodes), this caused kubelet to stall indefinitely during pod admission. The device manager now restricts iteration to NUMA nodes that actually host devices for the requested resource, reducing the search space to O(2^k) where k is typically 1–2.
```

issue_title: kubelet does not start when kubelet folder is on ZFS filesystem because of missing cadvisor plugin in 1.36.0
pr_title: re-introduce cadvisor ZFS support to kubelet
combined_text:
kubelet does not start when kubelet folder is on ZFS filesystem because of missing cadvisor plugin in 1.36.0 ### What happened?

After upgrading to Kubernetes 1.36.0, the kubelet crashes directly after startup on systems that have the `/var/lib/kubelet` folder configured on a ZFS filesystem. The direct error shown in the logs is:

```
E0423 21:45:14.682963  326484 kubelet.go:1821] "Failed to start ContainerManager" err="failed to get rootfs info: failed to get device for dir \"/var/lib/kubelet\": with major: 0, minor: 60: could not find device in cached partitions map"
```

This error is caused by the kubelet not being able to determine the available space for empheral storage, which it normally gets using cadvisor.

After increasing the log level, it seems that this is caused by cadvisor not having access to its ZFS plugin:
```
Apr 23 21:45:14 strike01 kubelet[326484]: I0423 21:45:14.680361  326484 fs.go:406] no plugin found for filesystem type: zfs
```

### What did you expect to happen?

Like Kubernetes 1.35.3 (or 1.35.4 for that matter): I expect the kubelet to startup and work.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

As described above: Ensure `/var/lib/kubelet` is on a ZFS filesystem and start the kubelet with any configuration.

### Anything else we need to know?

I am aware that Kubernetes does not maintain cadvisor and that cadvisor bugs should not be posted here. However, I believe that cadvisor is working correctly, it is just missing its ZFS plugin.

FS plug-ins in cadvisor are introduced in version v0.55.0, before the introduction everything was built-in. Kubernetes 1.36.0 brings cadvisor from version [v0.53.0 → v0.56.0](https://github.com/google/cadvisor/compare/v0.53.0...v0.56.0). It was specifically introduced in https://github.com/google/cadvisor/commit/28ecaa9d9b4aa2ea878f7692a315797c2a9d7a7c

In the PR merging 0.55.1, #135829, I see that some file system extensions are specifically being imported (https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/135829/changes#diff-ca41d4fa89358cfbf866e71de1c54ab978a448e805495bf8bca923d85dae31fb), but the ZFS plug-in is missing.

issue_title: "kube-proxy iptables mode has 5-6x performance regression when switching to iptables-nft backend"
pr_title: kube-proxy: don't do full periodic syncs on large cluster mode
combined_text:
"kube-proxy iptables mode has 5-6x performance regression when switching to iptables-nft backend" ### What happened?

kube-proxy iptables mode sync latency is 5-6x slower on aws-k8s-1.34 nodes compared to aws-k8s-1.32/1.33 nodes.

  | Node Variant | iptables version             | Rule Sync Latency | iptables-save time |
  |--------------|------------------------------|-------------------|--------------------|
  | aws-k8s-1.32 | iptables v1.8.11 (legacy)    | 32-52ms           | 21ms               |
  | aws-k8s-1.33 | iptables v1.8.11 (legacy)    | 24-34ms           | -                  |
  | aws-k8s-1.34 | iptables v1.8.11 (nf_tables) | 172-248ms         | 61ms               |

  Test environment: Same EKS cluster , same kube-proxy version (v1.34.3-eksbuild.2), same workload (877 services, 2057 endpoints).

  Root cause: aws-k8s-1.34 switched from legacy iptables to iptables-nft (nftables kernel backend with iptables compatibility layer). Every iptables operation is now translated to nftables, adding significant overhead.

  Evidence from the nodes:
  # aws-k8s-1.32 (fast)
  $ iptables --version
  iptables v1.8.11 (legacy)

  $ ls -la /usr/sbin/iptables*
  # Points to legacy iptables binaries

  # aws-k8s-1.34 (slow)
  $ iptables --version
  iptables v1.8.11 (nf_tables)

  $ ls -la /usr/sbin/iptables*
  lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root  7 Jan 23 01:35 /usr/sbin/iptables -> xtables
  lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 17 Jan 23 01:35 /usr/sbin/iptables-nft -> xtables-nft-multi

### What did you expect to happen?

kube-proxy running in iptables mode should have similar rule sync performance on aws-k8s-1.34 nodes as on aws-k8s-1.32/1.33 nodes

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Create an EKS cluster with mixed node groups:
    - Node group A: Bottlerocket aws-k8s-1.32 AMI
    - Node group B: Bottlerocket aws-k8s-1.34 AMI
  2. Deploy kube-proxy in iptables mode (EKS default)
  3. Deploy enough services to generate meaningful load (100+ services)
  4. Check kube-proxy logs on both node types:
  kubectl logs -n kube-system <kube-proxy-pod> | grep "SyncProxyRules complete"


### Anything else we need to know?

**Image I'm using:**

  - Bottlerocket OS 1.54.0 (aws-k8s-1.34) - kernel 6.12.63
  - Bottlerocket OS 1.54.0 (aws-k8s-1.32) - kernel 6.1.159 (for comparison)
  - Bottlerocket OS 1.54.0 (aws-k8s-1.33) - kernel 6.12.63 (for comparison)

 kube-proxy: don't do full periodic syncs on large cluster mode Periodic full-syncs are just reconcile loops just in case somehow the dataplane has drifted, however, they have an important cost on large clusters.

We can avoid to perform periodics full-sync if kube-proxy is in the "largecluster" mode, we are already doing some optimization on that mode, so it is reasonable to avoid the penalty of a full sync for a "just in case" operation.

/kind bug

```release-note
kube-proxy does not perform full-sync operations when operation in large cluster mode (more than 1000 endpoints)
```

Fixes: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/137085

/sig network
/assign @danwinship

combined_text: Failure cluster [99403658...] TestEvictDuringNamespaceTerminating failures https://storage.googleapis.com/k8s-triage/index.html?pr=1&text=TestEvictDuringNamespaceTerminating ### Failure cluster [994036583f9bc48a9f6f](https://go.k8s.io/triage#994036583f9bc48a9f6f) ##### Error text: ``` Failed === RUN TestEvictDuringNamespaceTerminating/Pod_refreshed_after_NamespaceTerminating_error evicting pod default/test-pod error when evicting pod "test-pod" from terminating namespace "default" (will retry after 5ms): pods "test-pod" is forbidden: namespace is terminating evicting pod default/test-pod drain_test.go:632: expected no error, got: error when evicting pods/"test-pod" -n "default": global timeout reached: 10ms --- FAIL: TestEvictDuringNamespaceTerminating/Pod_refreshed_after_NamespaceTerminating_error (0.01s) === RUN TestEvictDuringNamespaceTerminating --- FAIL: TestEvictDuringNamespaceTerminating (0.03s) ``` #### Recent failures: [3/13/2026, 2:26:12 PM ci-kubernetes-unit-s390x](https://prow.k8s.io/view/gs/kubernetes-ci-logs/logs/ci-kubernetes-unit-s390x/2032523443571789824) [3/10/2026, 4:01:49 AM pr:pull-kubernetes-unit](https://prow.k8s.io/view/gs/kubernetes-ci-logs/pr-logs/pull/137583/pull-kubernetes-unit/2031279218716839936) [3/6/2026, 9:12:03 PM ci-kubernetes-unit-dependencies](https://prow.k8s.io/view/gs/kubernetes-ci-logs/logs/ci-kubernetes-unit-dependencies/2030104043270967296) [3/3/2026, 3:34:14 AM ci-kubernetes-unit-s390x](https://prow.k8s.io/view/gs/kubernetes-ci-logs/logs/ci-kubernetes-unit-s390x/2028750610538434560) [3/2/2026, 6:26:26 PM ci-kubernetes-unit-s390x](https://prow.k8s.io/view/gs/kubernetes-ci-logs/logs/ci-kubernetes-unit-s390x/2028612712606470144) /kind failing-test kubectl/drain: widen namespace termination retry timeout #### What type of PR is this? /kind failing-test /sig cli #### What this PR does / why we need it: `TestEvictDuringNamespaceTerminating` intentionally exercises the retry path after a `NamespaceTerminating` eviction failure, but the subtest only gives the whole operation 10 milliseconds. The production loop sleeps, refreshes the pod, and retries under that same deadline, so one retry plus ordinary scheduler jitter is enough to make the test fail even when the behavior is correct. This change keeps the retry interval small and widens the overall timeout so the test checks the retry semantics instead of machine speed. #### Which issue(s) this PR is related to: Related to #137739 #### Special notes for your reviewer: - This targets the exact failing subtest from the issue: `TestEvictDuringNamespaceTerminating/Pod_refreshed_after_NamespaceTerminating_error`. - The observed failure is `global timeout reached: 10ms` after the retry path refreshes the pod. - Verified with: - `/opt/homebrew/bin/bash ./hack/verify-gofmt.sh` - `/opt/homebrew/bin/bash ./hack/verify-typecheck.sh ./staging/src/k8s.io/kubectl/pkg/drain/...` - `go test -race ./staging/src/k8s.io/kubectl/pkg/drain -run TestEvictDuringNamespaceTerminating -count=100` #### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change? ```release-note NONE ``` #### Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals), usage docs, etc.: ```docs ```

pr:pull-kubernetes-unit](https://prow.k8s.io/view/gs/kubernetes-ci-logs/pr-logs/pull/136585/pull-kubernetes-unit/2029937119236984832) /kind failing-test nodeipam: buffer TestNodeSyncResync report notifications #### What type of PR is this? /kind failing-test /sig network #### What this PR does / why we need it: `TestNodeSyncResync` closes `opChan` after observing the first resync and then waits for the loop to exit. There is still a small race where the 1 millisecond resync timer fires again before the select notices the closed channel. When that happens, `ReportResult` sends a second notification on an unbuffered `reportChan`, the loop blocks in that send, and the test hangs until the 5 minute timeout. This change gives `reportChan` one slot of buffering so the loop can drain that extra notification and then observe the closed channel. #### Which issue(s) this PR is related to: Related to #137740 #### Special notes for your reviewer: - This matches the current open issue and the earlier history in #134563 and #135217, but fixes the still-observable blocked-send path. - Verified with: - `/opt/homebrew/bin/bash ./hack/verify-gofmt.sh` - `/opt/homebrew/bin/bash ./hack/verify-typecheck.sh ./pkg/controller/nodeipam/ipam/sync/...` - `go test -race ./pkg/controller/nodeipam/ipam/sync -run TestNodeSyncResync -count=200` #### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change? ```release-note NONE ``` #### Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals), usage docs, etc.: ```docs ```


### Since when has it been failing?

Observed in job run [2034607623600345088](https://gcsweb.k8s.io/gcs/kubernetes-ci-logs/logs/ci-node-crio-swap-serial/2034607623600345088/artifacts/e2-standard-2-fedora-coreos-43-20260301-3-1-gcp-x86-64-117cbc54/).

### Reason for failure (if possible)

> **Note:** The following analysis is AI-summarized from the linked CI logs and source code.

The kubelet fatally crashes during restart when `userNamespaces.idsPerPod` is changed and a pod created with the previous `idsPerPod` value still has its directory on disk.

**Failing log line** (from [kubelet.log](https://gcsweb.k8s.io/gcs/kubernetes-ci-logs/logs/ci-node-crio-swap-serial/2034607623600345088/artifacts/e2-standard-2-fedora-coreos-43-20260301-3-1-gcp-x86-64-117cbc54/kubelet.log)):
```
E0319 12:51:56.731779  8547 run.go:72] "command failed" err="failed to run Kubelet: failed to create kubelet:
  create user namespace manager: record pod mappings for existing pod \"5982517a-d29a-4eae-887d-773f1f8a0548\":
  wrong user namespace length 131072"
```

**Sequence of events:**

1. The test sets `userNamespaces.idsPerPod = 131072` (65536 × 2) and restarts the kubelet.
2. A pod with `hostUsers: false` is created. The kubelet allocates a user namespace mapping with `length = 131072` and persists it to the pod's `userns` file on disk.
3. The pod succeeds and the test deletes it.
4. The AfterEach restores the original kubelet config (default `idsPerPod = 65536`) and restarts the kubelet.
5. During initialization, `MakeUserNsManager()` scans existing pod directories on disk (`pkg/kubelet/userns/userns_manager.go:185-190`). It finds the not-yet-cleaned-up pod directory, reads its `userns` file (which has `length = 131072`), and calls `record()`.
6. `record()` (`pkg/kubelet/userns/userns_manager.go:242-243`) performs a strict equality check:
   ```go
   if length != m.userNsLength {
       return fmt.Errorf("wrong user namespace length %v", length)
   }
   ```
   This fails because `131072 != 65536`, and the error is fatal — the kubelet never starts.
7. The AfterEach times out after 120s waiting for the kubelet health check, and the test is marked as FAILED.

**Impact:** This also means that outside of tests, changing `idsPerPod` in a kubelet config and restarting the kubelet will crash the kubelet if any pods using the old value still have directories on disk. The strict check in `record()` does not account for pods that were created with a different `idsPerPod`.

### Additional context

- [Full CI job logs](https://gcsweb.k8s.io/gcs/kubernetes-ci-logs/logs/ci-node-crio-swap-serial/2034607623600345088/artifacts/e2-standard-2-fedora-coreos-43-20260301-3-1-gcp-x86-64-117cbc54/)
- [build-log.txt](https://gcsweb.k8s.io/gcs/kubernetes-ci-logs/logs/ci-node-crio-swap-serial/2034607623600345088/build-log.txt)

/kind failing-test
/sig node tests: Wait for pod to be removed on kubelet restarts with userns.idsPerPod The test starts the kubelet with a non-default setting for idsPerPod, runs a pod, deletes it, and then restarts the kubelet.

The issue is that the kubelet guarantees that no two pods userns mappings overlap (for security reasons). But we are not waiting for the pod to be removed from the kubelet, the deleteSync() call only waits for the API server to remove the pod.

So, the pod is on disk (and maybe even running!) when we restart the kubelet. As the previous configuration is incompatible with the new one after restart if pods are running, the kubelet failing is the right thing. We should just wait for the pod to be deleted from the kubelet before restarting it with an incompatible configuration.

So, this commit just changes the pod deleteion (before done in e2eoutput.TestContainerOutput() just waiting for the API server) to wait for the kubelet to delete the pod.

issue_title: Add context to `"Watch failed"`
pr_title: client-go/tools/watch: downgrade 410 Gone error to V(4) debug log in RetryWatcher
combined_text:
Add context to `"Watch failed"` ### What would you like to be added?

Add some context to `retrywatcher.go`'s `Watch failed` error.

### Why is this needed?

A number of systems (especially argocd) experience a log message of the form:

```
1 retrywatcher.go:130] "Watch failed" err="context canceled"
```

## To Reproduce

Dunno

## Expected behavior

Log messages should give enough context for someone reading them to understand what's going on, i.e. "I tried to do _x_ and a watch failed".

## victims

general search: https://github.com/search?q=%22Watch+failed%22+kubernetes+retrywatcher.go&type=issues

https://github.com/argoproj/argo-cd/issues/14134#issue-1765671260
https://github.com/okteto/okteto/issues/3911#issue-1852069400
https://github.com/aws/eks-anywhere/issues/6535#issue-1857099062
https://github.com/devopstales/devopstales.github.io/issues/7#issuecomment-1059717657
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/118731#issuecomment-1596086603
https://github.com/open-cluster-management-io/ocm/issues/186#issue-1757987627

filed as https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/133555 client-go/tools/watch: downgrade 410 Gone error to V(4) debug log in RetryWatcher #### What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

When a `WatchWithContext` call returns a 410 Gone (resource expired) error, the
RetryWatcher logs it at ERROR level before retrying. Since this is an expected
and recoverable condition (e.g. resource version too old), it should not produce
ERROR-level log noise.

This PR adds an `apierrors.IsGone(err)` check before the catch-all `klog.Error`
call in the watch establishment error handling, downgrading the log to
`klog.V(4).Info` — consistent with how `IsProbableEOF` and `IsTimeout` are
already handled.

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

Fixes kubernetes/kubernetes#122207
Ref kubernetes/client-go#1321

#### Special notes for your reviewer:

The existing `watch.Error` event path (line 246, `http.StatusGone`) already
handles 410 Gone from the watch *stream*. This fix covers the case where
the `WatchWithContext` *establishment* call itself returns a Gone error.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
client-go: RetryWatcher now logs 410 Gone (resource expired) errors at debug verbosity (V(4)) instead of ERROR level during watch establishment.

```

#### Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals), usage docs, etc.:

NONE

issue_title: Cronjob controller try to get existed job without namespace
pr_title: Fix empty namespace when fetching existing job in cronjob controller
combined_text:
Cronjob controller try to get existed job without namespace ### What happened?

We're seeing when the senario in [cronjob_controllerv2.go#L615-L637](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/65f09e605cb206b2e5fcff4d69a4ae8acf62dbc3/pkg/controller/cronjob/cronjob_controllerv2.go#L615-L637) happened (Cronjob Controller found an existed job), Cronjob controller will try to get the job without specifying `namespace` value and get HTTP 404:


#### kube-controller-manager log:
```
E0210 18:47:10.835604       1 cronjob_controllerv2.go:179] "Unhandled Error" err="error syncing CronJobController default/sleep-300-job, requeuing: the server could not find the requested resource" logger="UnhandledError"
```

#### API Server log:
```
{
    "kind": "Event",
    "apiVersion": "audit.k8s.io/v1",
    "level": "RequestResponse",
    "auditID": "db48fd5c-6a37-4639-a6f0-2fcfeb0b5e8b",
    "stage": "ResponseComplete",

    # this doesn't contain namespace
    =====================
    "requestURI": "/apis/batch/v1/jobs/sleep-300-job-29512485",
    =====================

    "verb": "get",
    "user": {
        "username": "system:serviceaccount:kube-system:cronjob-controller",
        "uid": "31925f58-483c-4662-a3cf-3b9691279e43",
        "groups": [
            "system:serviceaccounts",
            "system:serviceaccounts:kube-system",
            "system:authenticated"
        ],
        "extra": {
            "authentication.kubernetes.io/credential-id": [
                "JTI=b4d33275-b6a9-4266-84ef-6ed222c2414e"
            ]
        }
    },
    "sourceIPs": [
        "192.168.130.2"
    ],
    "userAgent": "kube-controller-manager/v1.35.0 (linux/amd64) kubernetes/6645204/system:serviceaccount:kube-system:cronjob-controller",
    "objectRef": {
        "resource": "jobs",
        "name": "sleep-300-job-29512485",
        "apiGroup": "batch",
        "apiVersion": "v1"
    },
    "responseStatus": {
        "metadata": {},
        "status": "Failure",
        "message": "the server could not find the requested resource",
        "reason": "NotFound",
        "details": {},
        "code": 404
    },
    "responseObject": {
        "kind": "Status",
        "apiVersion": "v1",
        "metadata": {},
        "status": "Failure",
        "message": "the server could not find the requested resource",
        "reason": "NotFound",
        "details": {},
        "code": 404
    },
    "requestReceivedTimestamp": "2026-02-10T18:47:10.834353Z",
    "stageTimestamp": "2026-02-10T18:47:10.835086Z",
    "annotations": {
        "authorization.k8s.io/decision": "allow",
        "authorization.k8s.io/reason": "RBAC: allowed by ClusterRoleBinding \"system:controller:cronjob-controller\" of ClusterRole \"system:controller:cronjob-controller\" to ServiceAccount \"cronjob-controller/kube-system\""
    }
}
```

The issue was the "requestURI": `/apis/batch/v1/jobs/sleep-300-job-29512485` is correct, it should contain `namespace` e.g.:
```
❯ kubectl get job sleep-300-job-29512485 -v 9                                      
...
I0210 18:52:07.314585   31290 round_trippers.go:632] "Response" verb="GET" url="https://192.168.130.2:8443/apis/batch/v1/namespaces/default/jobs/sleep-300-job-29512485" status="200 OK" headers=<
```

And the API Server's 404 indicated there's no `/apis/batch/v1/jobs/` API.

### What did you expect to happen?

Cronjob controller should query the job `with namespace`:

Incorrect URI (current):
```
/apis/batch/v1/jobs/<JOB_NAME>
```

Correct URI:
```
/apis/batch/v1/namespaces/<NAMESPACE>/jobs/<JOB_NAME>
```

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Create a cronjob to create a job every 5 minutes:
```
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: sleep-300-job
spec:
  schedule: "*/5 * * * *"
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
            - name: echo-sleep
              image: ubuntu
              command:
                - /bin/bash
                - -c
                - echo start && sleep 180 && echo end
          restartPolicy: Never
```

Once the first job was created, create another job manually with the next expected job name (id + 5):
```
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: sleep-300-job-29512465
  namespace: default
  ownerReferences:
  - apiVersion: batch/v1
    blockOwnerDeletion: true
    controller: true
    kind: CronJob
    name: sleep-300-job
    uid: d10ff40f-be9d-47d2-aa67-7a376f054ed2
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - command:
        - /bin/bash
        - -c
        - echo start && sleep 180 && echo end
        image: ubuntu
        name: echo-sleep
      restartPolicy: Never
```

Check the kube-controller-manager and API Server audit log for the result.

### Anything else we need to know?

The issue seems to be when Cronjob controller trying to get job, it used the `jobReq` from `getJobFromTemplate2()` function, however, `getJobFromTemplate2()` won't set the job Namespace:
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/65f09e605cb206b2e5fcff4d69a4ae8acf62dbc3/pkg/controller/cronjob/cronjob_controllerv2.go#L621
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/65f09e605cb206b2e5fcff4d69a4ae8acf62dbc3/pkg/controller/cronjob/cronjob_controllerv2.go#L604
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/controller/cronjob/utils.go#L256-L263

Seems we're not relying on the `namespace` from `getJobFromTemplate2()`, it seems the correct way to specify `namespace` should be the same as:
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/65f09e605cb206b2e5fcff4d69a4ae8acf62dbc3/pkg/controller/cronjob/cronjob_controllerv2.go#L609


issue_title: [InPlacePodVerticalScaling] Move feasibility checks to admission
pr_title: [InPlacePodVerticalScaling] remove ineffectual static pod feasibility check
combined_text:
[InPlacePodVerticalScaling] Move feasibility checks to admission This is an umbrella issue to track the work of moving feasibility checks to admission.

Resizes can be considered infeasible for the following reasons:
- The requested resources exceed the node's total capacity.
- The pod is running on a node with an OS that does not support resize (like windows).
- The pod is a static pod.
- The feature gate is disabled on the node.
- Resize is not supported in combination with a few other features, which are:
  - Swap
  - Static CPU manager
  - Static memory manager


For infeasible resizes, the admission controller accepts the resize patch, with the feasibility check subsequently performed by the kubelet prior to resource allocation. If the resize is deemed infeasible, the kubelet adds a PodResizePending / Infeasible condition to the pod. An optimization we can make is to instead move these feasibility checks to admission, instead of accepting the patch and waiting for the kubelet to reject the resize.

The first 3 are trivial to move to admission. The remaining ones can be handled using the node declared features framework once we have commitment to implement those features so that we have a feature gate that we can tie the NDFs to.

/cc @tallclair @pravk03 
/assign

/sig node [InPlacePodVerticalScaling] remove ineffectual static pod feasibility check #### What type of PR is this?

/kind cleanup

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Remove the "static pod" feasibility check for pod resizes. It doesn't hurt to have the check here, but it doesn't do anything:
- The admission controller already blocks resize requests for mirror pods
- The kubelet treats modification of an underlying static pod manifest on the node as a "delete" followed by a "create", so resizing static pods in this way is permitted. I tried this on my own cluster to confirm.

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

Related: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/135341

issue_title: Distinguish PDB error separately in eviction API
pr_title: pkg/registry/core/pod/storage: add PDB-specific CauseType to eviction forbidden errors
combined_text:
Distinguish PDB error separately in eviction API ### What would you like to be added?

Currently in eviction API, when violating PDB, we'll have two common errors, see https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/eb6840928df59bf8203b1eda839ccd3da68fb37d/pkg/registry/core/pod/storage/eviction.go#L423-L432

We can't easily distinguish them with other `forbidden` or `tooManyRequests` errors, we do have different messages but that's not an appropriate criteria for judgement.

When detecting `update conflicts`, we have a conflict reason for it, maybe we can do the same thing here like **A Forbidden status code with different Reasons**, have no idea whether this is acceptable. but at least it's feasible because we maintain the status reasons ourself in kubernetes.

/kind api-machineary

### Why is this needed?

It would be great if we can tell them when calling eviction API, like in https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/pull/4329#discussion_r1637925556, this can be an important metric for users to know the reason why Pods can't be evicted, because of PDB constraints or others.  pkg/registry/core/pod/storage: add PDB-specific CauseType to eviction forbidden errors #### What type of PR is this?
```
/kind feature
/kind api-change
```

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

When `checkAndDecrement` returns a `Forbidden` error due to negative `DisruptionsAllowed` or an oversized `DisruptedPods` map, clients had no programmatic way to distinguish these from other `Forbidden` errors without string-matching on the message.

This PR adds two new `CauseType` constants to `policy/v1`:
- `DisruptionBudgetNegativeAllowedDisruptions` — for the `DisruptionsAllowed < 0` case
- `DisruptionBudgetTooManyDisruptedPods` — for the `DisruptedPods` map overflow case

And populates `StatusCause` on both `Forbidden` error paths in `checkAndDecrement`, consistent with the existing pattern already used on the `TooManyRequests` path via `DisruptionBudgetCause`.

Clients can now use `errors.HasStatusCause(err, policyv1.DisruptionBudgetNegativeAllowedDisruptions)` for reliable, typed, machine-readable error discrimination, no message parsing needed.

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
Fixes #125500

#### Special notes:

The two new `CauseType` constants follow the same naming convention and placement as the existing `DisruptionBudgetCause` constant in `staging/src/k8s.io/api/policy/v1/types.go`. No codegen changes were required since `CauseType` is a `string` alias.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
Added structured `CauseType` values to PodDisruptionBudget-related eviction `Forbidden` errors in the eviction API, allowing clients to programmatically distinguish PDB invalid-state errors from other forbidden errors without string-matching on the message.
```

```console

vllm-opt-125m-6b6f655ddb-8zb4p   1776286635235007008   model
```

This shouldn't be happening. Once the resource claims are created, they should be added there and it should remain stable.

### What did you expect to happen?

Once the resource claims are created, they should be added there and it should remain stable. Here's an example of a Pod that has reached `Running` state:


I suspect this happens whenever a pod is in pending and uses multiple ResourceClaims, but I am not sure. I have a unit test that reproduces it, but not an e2e test. I will submit a PR with the unit test and a fix.

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

issue_title: KEP-5304: requests with count>1 produce incorrect device metadata
pr_title: KEP-5304, fix: merge same request metadata across drivers
combined_text:
KEP-5304: requests with count>1 produce incorrect device metadata ### What happened?

When count>1 is used in resource claim spec and requests are allocated with different drivers, the consumer-side readRequestDir merge in k8s.io/dynamic-resource-allocation/devicemetadata produces duplicate DeviceMetadataRequest entries with the same Name rather than a single merged entry. Xref: https://github.com/kubernetes/dynamic-resource-allocation/blob/c46864948a5f87b6ba006b6823a6893cbe2bf064/devicemetadata/decode.go#L150-L153

### What did you expect to happen?

For this case a single entry in DeviceMetadata.Requests was expected.

Each entry in DeviceMetadata.Requests should be with a unique name. When the same request is used to allocate multiple devices potentially from different drivers, the devices should be merged into a single request.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Will add a unit test for this.

 KEP-5304, fix: merge same request metadata across drivers 
#### What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Fixes consumer-side metadata merge behavior in `devicemetadata.readRequestDir`.

When a single claim request is allocated across multiple drivers,
each driver writes its own `*-metadata.json` file. Before this
change, merging these files appended `Requests` entries directly, which could
produce duplicate request objects with the same `request.Name`.

This change merges by request name:

- if a request name is new, append it
- if it already exists, append only devices into the existing request entry

This guarantees one merged request entry per request name in the combined result.

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

Fixes https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/138352
KEP: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/5304

#### Special notes for your reviewer:

- Scope is intentionally minimal and focused on merge behavior for duplicate
  request names across multiple driver files.
