issue_title: Wrong "consider borrowing here" suggestion with `impl IntoIterator<Item = impl Borrow<str>>`
pr_title: Do not suggest borrowing enclosing calls for nested where-clause obligations
combined_text:
Wrong "consider borrowing here" suggestion with `impl IntoIterator<Item = impl Borrow<str>>` ### Code

```Rust
use std::borrow::Borrow;

fn foo(_v: impl IntoIterator<Item = impl Borrow<str>>) {}

fn main() {
    foo(&[String::from("a")]);
}
```

### Current output

```Shell
Compiling test-borrow v0.1.0 (/tmp/test-borrow)
error[E0277]: the trait bound `&String: Borrow<str>` is not satisfied
 --> src/main.rs:6:5
  |
6 |     foo(&[String::from("a")]);
  |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ the trait `Borrow<str>` is not implemented for `&String`
  |
note: required by a bound in `foo`
 --> src/main.rs:3:42
  |
3 | fn foo(_v: impl IntoIterator<Item = impl Borrow<str>>) {}
  |                                          ^^^^^^^^^^^ required by this bound in `foo`
help: consider borrowing here
  |
6 |     &foo(&[String::from("a")]);
  |     +

For more information about this error, try `rustc --explain E0277`.
error: could not compile `test-borrow` (bin "test-borrow") due to 1 previous error
```

### Desired output

```Shell
Compiling test-borrow v0.1.0 (/tmp/test-borrow)
error[E0277]: the trait bound `&String: Borrow<str>` is not satisfied
 --> src/main.rs:6:5
  |
6 |     foo(&[String::from("a")]);
  |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ the trait `Borrow<str>` is not implemented for `&String`
  |
note: required by a bound in `foo`
 --> src/main.rs:3:42
  |
3 | fn foo(_v: impl IntoIterator<Item = impl Borrow<str>>) {}
  |                                          ^^^^^^^^^^^ required by this bound in `foo`

For more information about this error, try `rustc --explain E0277`.
error: could not compile `test-borrow` (bin "test-borrow") due to 1 previous error
```

### Rationale and extra context

If you apply the suggestion, it keeps suggestion adding more `&`'s:
```
help: consider borrowing here
  |
6 |     &&foo(&[String::from("a")]);
  |      +
```

```
help: consider borrowing here
  |
6 |     &&&foo(&[String::from("a")]);
  |       +
```

```
help: consider borrowing here
  |
6 |     &&&&foo(&[String::from("a")]);
  |        +
```

### Other cases

```Rust

```

### Rust Version

```Shell
$ rustc --version --verbose
rustc 1.94.1 (e408947bf 2026-03-25)
binary: rustc
commit-hash: e408947bfd200af42db322daf0fadfe7e26d3bd1
commit-date: 2026-03-25
host: x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu
release: 1.94.1
LLVM version: 21.1.8
$ rustc +nightly --version --verbose
rustc 1.96.0-nightly (f5eca4fcf 2026-04-09)
binary: rustc
commit-hash: f5eca4fcfa908d1e038afd19c6e746f075859130
commit-date: 2026-04-09
host: x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu
release: 1.96.0-nightly
LLVM version: 22.1.2
```

### Anything else?

_No response_ Do not suggest borrowing enclosing calls for nested where-clause obligations In rust-lang/rust#155088, the compiler was blaming the whole call expr instead of the value that  actually failed the trait bound, so for foo(&[String::from("a")]) it was suggesting stuff like &foo(...). I changed the suggestion logic so it only emits borrow help if the expr it found actually matches the failed self type,  and used the same check for the “similar impl exists” help too. So now the compiler should give the normal error + required bound note. 

Fix rust-lang/rust#155088

issue_title: -Zmir-opt-level >= 3 errors with recursive async
pr_title: Avoid query cycles in DataflowConstProp
combined_text:
-Zmir-opt-level >= 3 errors with recursive async If you try compiling something that includes a recursive async function with -Zmir-opt-level=3 or higher, rustc gives an error.

```
pub async fn foo(n: usize) {
    if n > 0 {
        Box::pin(foo(n - 1)).await;
    }
}
```

```
error[E0733]: recursion in an async fn requires boxing
 --> src/main.rs:1:1
  |
1 | pub async fn foo(n: usize) {
  | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  |
  = note: a recursive `async fn` call must introduce indirection such as `Box::pin` to avoid an infinitely sized future
```

It looks like the responsible pass is DataflowConstProp where coroutines should probably be skipped like others do. Avoid query cycles in DataflowConstProp <!-- homu-ignore:start -->
<!--
If this PR is related to an unstable feature or an otherwise tracked effort,
please link to the relevant tracking issue here. If you don't know of a related
tracking issue or there are none, feel free to ignore this.

This PR will get automatically assigned to a reviewer. In case you would like
a specific user to review your work, you can assign it to them by using

    r? <reviewer name>
-->
<!-- homu-ignore:end -->

Fixes rust-lang/rust#155376 by skipping coroutines.

@rustbot label +F-const_closures Fix ICE when const closure appears inside a non-const trait method Fixes rust-lang/rust#153891 `hir_body_const_context()` unconditionally delegated to the parent's const context for const closures, returning `None` when the parent had no const context. This caused `mir_const_qualif()` to hit a `span_bug!`, since `mir_promoted()` had already decided to call it based on the closure's own syntactic constness. Fall back to `ConstContext::ConstFn` when the parent's const context is `None`, so that the const closure body is still properly const-checked rather than triggering an ICE. Examining [another attempt](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/153900/changes) at this issue (which has already been closed), I thought that its approach represents a workaround fix to avoid inconsistencies in the caller of `mir_promoted()`, whereas I think the correct behavior is for `hir_body_const_context()` itself to return the proper value.

issue_title: E0308 mismatched type on trait impl with different concrete types
pr_title: Improve E0308 error message for `impl Trait` return mismatches
combined_text:
E0308 mismatched type on trait impl with different concrete types ### Code

```Rust
trait Value {}

struct First {}
impl Value for First {}

struct Second {}
impl Value for Second {}

fn get_value(first: bool) -> impl Value {
    if first {
        return First {};
    }
    Second {}
}
```

### Current output

```Shell
Compiling playground v0.0.1 (/playground)
error[E0308]: mismatched types
  --> src/lib.rs:13:5
   |
 9 | fn get_value(first: bool) -> impl Value {
   |                              ---------- expected `First` because of return type
...
13 |     Second {}
   |     ^^^^^^^^^ expected `First`, found `Second`

For more information about this error, try `rustc --explain E0308`.
error: could not compile `playground` (lib) due to 1 previous error
```

### Desired output

```Shell
Compiling playground v0.0.1 (/playground)
error[E0308]: mismatched types
  --> src/lib.rs:13:5
   |
 9 | fn get_value(first: bool) -> impl Value {
   |                              ---------- expected a single type implementing `Value` because of return type
...
   |
11 |         return First {};
   |                ^^^^^ return type resolved to be `First`
...
13 |     Second {}
   |     ^^^^^^^^^ expected `First`, found `Second`

For more information about this error, try `rustc --explain E0308`.
error: could not compile `playground` (lib) due to 1 previous error
```

### Rationale and extra context

Even though I had read the entire reference, I forgot about the [rule ](https://doc.rust-lang.org/1.94.1/reference/types/impl-trait.html#r-type.impl-trait.return.constraint-body) that

> Each possible return value from the function must resolve to the same concrete type.

And the existing message suggests that the return type in the signature is what breaks the `Second {}` return, but it isn't - instead, it is the fact that `First {}` is returned above

### Other cases

```Rust

```

### Rust Version

```Shell
1.94.1
```

### Anything else?

_No response_

<!-- TRIAGEBOT_START -->

<!-- TRIAGEBOT_ASSIGN_START -->

<!-- TRIAGEBOT_ASSIGN_END -->
<!-- TRIAGEBOT_END --> Improve E0308 error message for `impl Trait` return mismatches When a function returns `impl Trait`, all branches must return the same concrete type. Previously, the compiler showed:

    expected `First` because of return type

This was misleading, as it suggested the return type was `First`, rather than any single type implementing the trait.

Update the diagnostic to:

    expected a single type implementing `Value` because of return type

Also highlight the first return expression to make it clearer why subsequent returns do not match.

issue_title: Reassigning table_name on a model causes find/first/where.to_a to get progressively slower — O(N²) cost (7.1 - 8.1+)
pr_title: Fix duplicate entries accumulating in `aliases_by_attribute_name`
combined_text:
Reassigning table_name on a model causes find/first/where.to_a to get progressively slower — O(N²) cost (7.1 - 8.1+) ### Steps to reproduce

```ruby
require "bundler/inline"

gemfile do
  source "https://rubygems.org"
  gem "activerecord", "~> 8.1"
  gem "sqlite3"
  gem "benchmark"
end

require "active_record"
require "benchmark"

ActiveRecord::Base.establish_connection(adapter: "sqlite3", database: ":memory:")

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table(:users) { |t| t.string :name }
  create_table(:posts) { |t| t.string :title }
end

ActiveRecord::Base.connection.execute("INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ('x')")
ActiveRecord::Base.connection.execute("INSERT INTO posts (title) VALUES ('y')")

klass = Class.new(ActiveRecord::Base)

8.times do |i|
  t = Benchmark.realtime do
    50.times do
      klass.table_name = "users"
      klass.first
      klass.table_name = "posts"
      klass.first
    end
  end
  size = klass.send(:aliases_by_attribute_name)["id"]&.size || 0
  puts format("iteration %d: %7.1fms, aliases_by_attribute_name['id'].size=%d", i, t * 1000, size)
end
```

The loop does only four things per inner iteration: reassign `table_name`, query, reassign `table_name` to the other table, query.

### Expected

Each iteration takes roughly the same time. `aliases_by_attribute_name["id"]` contains a single entry (`"id_value"`) regardless of how many times `table_name` has been reassigned.

### Actual

Per-iteration wall time grows linearly — total cost across N iterations is quadratic. `aliases_by_attribute_name["id"]` grows unbounded by one entry per `table_name=` followed by any record-instantiating query (`find`, `first`, `last`, `where(...).to_a`, etc.).

Output on activerecord 8.1.3 / ruby 4.0.2:

```
iteration 0:   568.8ms, aliases_by_attribute_name['id'].size=100
iteration 1:  1541.9ms, aliases_by_attribute_name['id'].size=200
iteration 2:  2549.8ms, aliases_by_attribute_name['id'].size=300
iteration 3:  3623.2ms, aliases_by_attribute_name['id'].size=400
iteration 4:  4619.1ms, aliases_by_attribute_name['id'].size=500
iteration 5:  5458.5ms, aliases_by_attribute_name['id'].size=600
iteration 6:  6496.9ms, aliases_by_attribute_name['id'].size=700
iteration 7:  7408.3ms, aliases_by_attribute_name['id'].size=800
```

Per-iteration time grew ~13× over 8 iterations of identical work. No stabilization — the slope continues indefinitely.

### Root cause

`ActiveRecord::AttributeMethods::ClassMethods#define_attribute_methods` (introduced in 7.1) registers `alias_attribute :id_value, :id` as part of attribute-method generation. Reassigning `table_name` invalidates the generated methods, so the next record instantiation re-runs `define_attribute_methods`, which re-registers the alias.

`ActiveModel::AttributeMethods::ClassMethods#alias_attribute` appends to `aliases_by_attribute_name[old_name]` without checking for duplicates:

```ruby
aliases_by_attribute_name[old_name] << new_name
```

So every `table_name=` followed by a record-instantiating query appends another `"id_value"` to `aliases_by_attribute_name["id"]`. Subsequent attribute-method generation iterates this array, producing the quadratic growth observed above.

### Scope

The accumulation fires on any query that instantiates an `ActiveRecord::Base` record: `find`, `first`, `last`, `where(...).to_a`, `find_each`, etc. It does **not** fire on aggregate or raw-value queries — `count`, `exists?`, `pluck`, or `where(...)` alone (which returns a lazy relation). So code that only touches these escapes the regression.

### Real-world impact

Encountered while investigating a performance regression in [Stagehand](https://github.com/combinaut/stagehand), a staging-to-production data-sync library. Its `Stagehand::Production::Record` class reassigns `table_name` per synced record to read from and delete from many different tables through a single AR class. After the 7.1 upgrade, per-iteration wall time in a sync of 3000 entries grew from ~22ms (first batch of 1000) to ~210ms (third batch of 1000) — CPU-bound, not I/O-bound. Total runtime: ~3.5 minutes instead of seconds. Any code that swaps `table_name` on a persistent class between queries will exhibit the same shape.

### System configuration

Rails version: 8.1.3 (reproducible on 7.1+)
Ruby version: 4.0.2

### Upstream fix

#57227 addresses this by deduplicating in `alias_attribute`. Filing this issue to document the regression and attach real-world impact, so it's discoverable by anyone hitting the same symptom before the fix lands.
 Fix duplicate entries accumulating in `aliases_by_attribute_name` Fixes #57235

### Motivation / Background

`ActiveModel::AttributeMethods#alias_attribute` appends to `aliases_by_attribute_name[old_name]` without checking for duplicates. When the same alias is registered more than once on the same class, the underlying storage accumulates duplicate entries, and `define_attribute_methods` later iterates every duplicate — generating the same aliased attribute methods once per duplicate.

This is a regression for any code that reassigns `self.table_name = ...` on a reusable AR class. Since Rails 7.1, `ActiveRecord::Base#load_schema!` auto-calls `alias_attribute :id_value, :id` for every `id` column on every schema load ([`activerecord/lib/active_record/model_schema.rb#L628`](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/d39db5d1891f7509cde2efc425c9d69bbb77e670/activerecord/lib/active_record/model_schema.rb#L628), introduced in [rails/rails@e90b11e77e](https://github.com/rails/rails/commit/e90b11e77e)). Combined with the persistent `aliases_by_attribute_name` structure ([`activemodel/lib/active_model/attribute_methods.rb#L204-L209`](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/d39db5d1891f7509cde2efc425c9d69bbb77e670/activemodel/lib/active_model/attribute_methods.rb#L204-L209), introduced in [rails/rails@0f5563bd40](https://github.com/rails/rails/commit/0f5563bd40)), repeatedly setting `table_name` appends a new `"id_value"` entry on every schema reload. After N reassignments, `aliases_by_attribute_name["id"]` contains N duplicate `"id_value"` strings, and each `define_attribute_methods` call re-generates aliased accessor methods N times per pattern per column.

Rails 7.0 and earlier did not have either the auto-alias or the persistent storage, so this pattern was effectively free before 7.1.

Compare 7.0 vs 7.1 `alias_attribute`:
- 7.0.10: [`activemodel/lib/active_model/attribute_methods.rb#L204-L235`](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/f4321f78fe567fd29dd0a0fb5cb17af68efd36bb/activemodel/lib/active_model/attribute_methods.rb#L204-L235) — defines methods inline via `attribute_method_matchers.each`, no `aliases_by_attribute_name`.
- 7.1.0: [`activemodel/lib/active_model/attribute_methods.rb#L204-L209`](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/d39db5d1891f7509cde2efc425c9d69bbb77e670/activemodel/lib/active_model/attribute_methods.rb#L204-L209) — appends to `aliases_by_attribute_name` (L208), delegates generation to `define_attribute_methods`.

In a representative reproduction, per-query cost grew monotonically from ~12 ms to ~90 ms over ~1000 `table_name=` swaps on a single class, with per-call allocations climbing from ~41 k to ~246 k objects — all attributable to repeated `define_attribute_method_pattern` work firing exponentially more times as duplicates accumulated.

### Detail

Dedupe the append in `alias_attribute`:

```ruby
aliases = aliases_by_attribute_name[old_name]
aliases << new_name unless aliases.include?(new_name)
```

The rest of the method is unchanged. `attribute_aliases.merge(new_name => old_name)` is already idempotent, and `eagerly_generate_alias_attribute_methods` is safe to re-call because `define_attribute_method_pattern` early-returns on `instance_method_already_implemented?`.

### Additional information

Two tests are added:

1. An ActiveModel unit test (`activemodel/test/cases/attribute_methods_test.rb`) calling `alias_attribute :bar, :foo` three times on the same class and asserting `aliases_by_attribute_name["foo"] == ["bar"]`.
2. An ActiveRecord integration test (`activerecord/test/cases/attribute_methods_test.rb`) that mirrors the real-world regression path — an anonymous `ActiveRecord::Base` subclass with `table_name` reassigned between `topics` and `posts`, asserting `aliases_by_attribute_name["id"] == ["id_value"]` after three `.first` queries.

Both tests fail on unpatched `main` (clean diffs: `["bar"] expected, ["bar", "bar", "bar"] actual` and `["id_value"]` expected, `["id_value", "id_value"]` actual) and pass with the patch applied. Full `activemodel` suite (1168 tests) and full `activerecord` sqlite3 suite (9418 tests) pass with zero new failures.

### Checklist

Before submitting the PR make sure the following are checked:

* [x] This Pull Request is related to one change.
* [x] Commit message has a detailed description of what changed and why.
* [x] Tests are added for the bug fix.
* [ ] CHANGELOG files are updated for the changed libraries if there is a behavior change or additional feature. Minor bug fixes and documentation changes should not be included.

issue_title: Route Constraints passed via `mount` function override wrapping constraints with no Warning and behavior is not documented
pr_title: Fix innermost constraint reseting previous constraints
combined_text:
Route Constraints passed via `mount` function override wrapping constraints with no Warning and behavior is not documented ### Steps to reproduce

```
constraints SomeIPConstraint do
  mount Sidekiq::Web => '/sidekiq', :constraints => SomeReadOnlyConstraint
end
```

This became more of an issue when coupled with devise (`v4.9.4`) authenticate function ([source](https://github.com/heartcombo/devise/blob/731074bf09c2a0cd498c1b8a2a01434e722f94d5/lib/devise/rails/routes.rb#L289)), which uses constraints to implement the functionality.

```
constraints SomeIPConstraint do
  authenticate :admin_user, ->(user) { user.can_access_sidekiq? } do
    mount Sidekiq::Web => '/sidekiq', :constraints => SomeReadOnlyConstraint
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

If the design intention is for constraints passed directly to the mount method to override surrounding constraints blocks, we would expect a warning in the console or a note in the documentation.

OR

Constraints passed via the mount method should append to (or be composed with) any surrounding block-level constraints.

Apologies if I have overlooked an existing explanation for this. I am happy to raise a PR to address this using either approach mentioned above.

### Actual behavior

Only the `SomeReadOnlyConstraint` from the example above is evaluated. The `SomeIPConstraint` and the Devise authentication constraint are both disregarded.

There is currently no warning provided, and this behavior appears to be undocumented.

### System configuration

**Rails version**:  `7.2.2.2`

**Ruby version**:  `3.4.7`
 Fix innermost constraint reseting previous constraints 
### Motivation / Background

Fixes https://github.com/rails/rails/issues/56528 

This Pull Request has been created because having multiple constraints with nesting causes unexpected behaviour where only the innermost constraint "wins"

### Detail

This Pull Request changes ActionDispatch constraint merging to collect a list of constraint blocks.

issue_title: Dockerfile: duplicated libvips install
pr_title: Remove redundant libvips from Dockerfile build packages
combined_text:
Dockerfile: duplicated libvips install ### Steps to reproduce

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->
Create a new Rails app with Docker enabled and Active Storage image processing, then inspect the generated Dockerfile.


<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->
```
  rails new dockerfile-libvips-repro --database=postgresql
  cd dockerfile-libvips-repro
  grep -n "libvips" Dockerfile

  The generated Dockerfile installs libvips in both the base stage and the build stage, even though the build stage inherits from base:

  FROM docker.io/library/ruby:$RUBY_VERSION-slim AS base

  RUN apt-get update -qq && \
      apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y curl libjemalloc2 libvips postgresql-client && \
      rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists /var/cache/apt/archives

  FROM base AS build

  RUN apt-get update -qq && \
      apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y build-essential git libpq-dev libvips pkg-config libyaml-dev && \
      rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists /var/cache/apt/archives
```

### Expected behavior

<!-- Tell us what should happen -->
  libvips should only be installed in the base stage when the build stage inherits from base, unless there is a specific build-time package requirement that
  differs from the runtime package.

  The build-stage install should omit the duplicate package:
```
  RUN apt-get update -qq && \
      apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y build-essential git libpq-dev pkg-config libyaml-dev && \
      rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists /var/cache/apt/archives
```

### Actual behavior

<!-- Tell us what happens instead -->
  The generated Dockerfile installs libvips twice: once in base, and again in build.

  Since `FROM base AS build` already includes packages installed in base, the second install appears redundant. This increases build work and can make  generated production Dockerfiles look more complex than necessary.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.3

**Ruby version**: 4.0.1
 Remove redundant libvips from Dockerfile build packages ### Motivation / Background
This Pull Request has been created because libvips is included in the build packages and the runtime (base) packages. Since the build stage inherits from base, including it in the build packages resulted in a duplicate installation entry in the generated Dockerfile.

Fixes [#57237](https://github.com/rails/rails/issues/57237)

### Detail

This Pull Request changes railties/lib/rails/generators/app_base.rb

issue_title: Update documentation for `ActiveSupport::TimeZone#parse` to reflect that it returns `nil` for invalid strings
pr_title: Clarify TimeZone#parse docs for invalid strings
combined_text:
Update documentation for `ActiveSupport::TimeZone#parse` to reflect that it returns `nil` for invalid strings ### Steps to reproduce

```ruby
require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"
  gem "activesupport"
end

require "active_support/all"

# Set a time zone
Time.zone = "UTC"

# According to the documentation: 
# "If the string is invalid then an ArgumentError could be raised."
# https://api.rubyonrails.org/classes/ActiveSupport/TimeZone.html#method-i-parse

invalid_input = "invalid date string"
result = Time.zone.parse(invalid_input)

puts "Result: #{result.inspect}"

if result.nil?
  puts "Current behavior: returns nil"
else
  puts "Current behavior: returns #{result.class}"
end
```

### Expected behavior


issue_title: `reset_counters` silently sets counter to 0 when `id` is a String
pr_title: Fix `reset_counters` when using string ids
combined_text:
`reset_counters` silently sets counter to 0 when `id` is a String # `reset_counters` silently sets counter to 0 when `id` is a String

### Steps to reproduce

Save the following script as `reset_counters_bug.rb` and run it with `ruby reset_counters_bug.rb`:

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails", "~> 8.1.0"
  # To verify this works on 8.0, replace the line above with:
  # gem "rails", "~> 8.0.0"

  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :posts, force: true do |t|
    t.integer :comments_count, default: 0, null: false
  end

  create_table :comments, force: true do |t|
    t.integer :post_id
  end
end

class Post < ActiveRecord::Base
  has_many :comments
end

class Comment < ActiveRecord::Base
  belongs_to :post, counter_cache: true
end

class ResetCountersStringIdBugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_reset_counters_with_integer_id
    post = Post.create!
    5.times { post.comments.create! }

    # Manually desync the counter to simulate needing a reset
    Post.update_counters(post.id, comments_count: -post.comments_count)
    assert_equal 0, post.reload.comments_count

    # Reset with integer id — works correctly
    Post.reset_counters(post.id, :comments_count)
    assert_equal 5, post.reload.comments_count
  end

  def test_reset_counters_with_string_id
    post = Post.create!
    5.times { post.comments.create! }

    # Manually desync the counter to simulate needing a reset
    Post.update_counters(post.id, comments_count: -post.comments_count)
    assert_equal 0, post.reload.comments_count

    # Reset with string id (common when id comes from params) — FAILS on 8.1
    Post.reset_counters(post.id.to_s, :comments_count)
    assert_equal 5, post.reload.comments_count,
      "Expected comments_count to be 5 after reset_counters with string id, but got #{post.reload.comments_count}"
  end
end
```

**On Rails 8.0**: both tests pass.
**On Rails 8.1**: `test_reset_counters_with_string_id` fails — the counter stays at 0.

### Expected behavior

`reset_counters` should set the counter to the actual count of associated records regardless of whether the `id` argument is a String or Integer. This is the behavior in Rails 8.0 and earlier.

### Actual behavior

When `id` is passed as a String, `reset_counters` silently resets the counter to **0** instead of the actual count of associated records.

### Root cause

`reset_counters` was rewritten in Rails 8.1 to use a `GROUP BY` query instead of loading the object:

```ruby
# activerecord/lib/active_record/counter_cache.rb (Rails 8.1)
def reset_counters(id, *counters, touch: nil)
  ids = Array(id)  # ["123"] when id is a string

  counts = unscoped
    .joins(counter_association)
    .where(primary_key => ids)
    .group(primary_key)
    .count(:all)
  # Returns: {123 => 5} — integer keys from the database

  ids.each do |id|
    updates[id].merge!(counter_name => counts[id] || 0)
    # counts["123"] => nil — string key does not match integer key
    # Falls back to 0
  end
end
```

The `.group(primary_key).count(:all)` returns a hash with integer keys (the database returns integers for integer primary key columns), but the lookup uses the original string `id` from the `ids` array. Since `"123" != 123` in Ruby, the lookup returns `nil` and the fallback `|| 0` sets the counter to 0.

The previous implementation (Rails 8.0) avoided this by using `find(id)` which handles type coercion, then counting through the association proxy:

```ruby
# activerecord/lib/active_record/counter_cache.rb (Rails 8.0)
def reset_counters(id, *counters, touch: nil)
  object = find(id)  # find coerces string to integer
  # ...
  count = object.send(counter_association).count(:all)  # correct count
  updates[counter_name] = count
end
```

### Why this matters

Passing string ids to `reset_counters` is common in Rails applications — controller params are always strings, and the previous implementation handled this transparently for years. The silent nature of the bug (no error raised, counter quietly set to 0) makes it particularly hard to diagnose. The actual failure manifests much later when a counter cache decrement tries to go below 0 on an unsigned column, far from the code that caused the desync.

### System configuration

- **Rails version**: 8.1.3
- **Ruby version**: 4.0.2
- **Database**: MySQL 8.x (mysql2 adapter), reproduction script uses SQLite3

The issue affects any database adapter where `.group().count()` returns integer keys for integer primary key columns. Fix `reset_counters` when using string ids Fixes #56122.
Fixes #57185.
Closes #56123.

### System configuration **Rails version**: 8.1.3 **Ruby version**: 3.3.7 Fix io copy stream raises type error when writing ### Motivation / Background Fixes: https://github.com/rails/rails/issues/57186 `IO.copy_stream(src, response.stream)` raised a `TypeError: no implicit conversion of Array into Integer` when writing to Rails response stream buffers. This is because `Buffer#write` was returning the buffer array (result of `Array#push`) instead of an Integer representing bytes written, which is what `IO.copy_stream` expects from a destination `write` method. A second, silent data-corruption bug was also present: `IO.copy_stream` reuses its internal string buffer after each call to `write` returns. Since `Buffer#write` stored the passed string by reference rather than duplicating it first, the buffered content could be mutated after being enqueued, silently corrupting the response body. Both `ActionDispatch::Response::Buffer#write` and `ActionController::Live::Buffer#write` were affected. ### Detail This Pull Request changes `Buffer#write` in two places: **`ActionDispatch::Response::Buffer#write`** (`actionpack/lib/action_dispatch/http/response.rb`): - Pushes `string.dup` onto the buffer instead of the original reference, preventing `IO.copy_stream`'s internal buffer reuse from corrupting already-enqueued chunks. - Returns `string.bytesize` so that `IO.copy_stream` (and any other `IO`-compatible consumer) receives the expected Integer return value. **`ActionController::Live::Buffer#write`** (`actionpack/lib/action_controller/metal/live.rb`): - Returns `string.bytesize` for the same reason. Tests are added for both classes to verify: 1. `#write` returns the correct byte count. 2. `IO.copy_stream` produces the correct response body without corruption. ### Additional information `IO.copy_stream` documents that the destination's `write` method must return the number of bytes written (an Integer). The root cause was that `Array#push` returns the array itself, which propagated back as the return value of `Buffer#write`, causing `IO.copy_stream` to raise immediately. issue_title: GET request with params keyword in IntegrationTest incorrectly routes as POST in Rails 8.1.3
pr_title: Fix GET+JSON+params in integration tests for API-only apps
combined_text:
GET request with params keyword in IntegrationTest incorrectly routes as POST in Rails 8.1.3 ### Steps to reproduce

Using Rails 8.1.3, create a minimal application with a single GET route and a corresponding controller action. Write an integration test that makes a GET request using the `params:` keyword argument with the `as: :json` option.

**Reproduction script (save as bug_report.rb):**

```ruby
require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"
  gem "rails", "8.1.3"
end

require "action_controller/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.root = __dir__
  config.secret_key_base = "secret"
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  Rails.logger = config.logger

  routes.draw do
    get "/items", to: "items#index"
    post "/items", to: "items#create"
  end
end

class ItemsController < ActionController::Base
  def index
    render plain: "GET successful with params: #{params[:filter]}"
  end

  def create
    render plain: "POST successful"
  end
end

require "minitest/autorun"

class BugTest < ActionDispatch::IntegrationTest
  def test_get_with_params_keyword_fails
    get "/items", params: { filter: "active" }, as: :json
    assert_response :success
  end

  def test_get_with_manual_query_string_succeeds
    get "/items?filter=active", as: :json
    assert_response :success
  end
end
```

**Run the script:**
```bash
ruby bug_report.rb
```

### Expected behavior

Both tests should pass. The `get` request with the `params:` keyword argument should correctly route to the `GET /items` route and return a success response.

### Actual behavior

The test `test_get_with_params_keyword_fails` fails with a routing error:

```
Expected response to be a <2XX: success>, but was a <404: Not Found>
Response body: {"status":404,"error":"Not Found","exception":"#<ActionController::RoutingError: No route matches [POST] \"/items\">"}
```

The router incorrectly interprets the GET request as a POST request when the `params:` keyword is used with `as: :json`.

The second test using a manually constructed query string passes as expected.

### Additional context

This issue affects Rails 8.1.3. It appears to be related to how the test helper encodes the `params:` hash into the request body for GET requests. When a GET request includes a body (non-standard but technically allowed), the Rails router may misinterpret the request method or route it incorrectly.

The problem manifests in real-world projects where multiple controller tests fail with the same pattern. The workaround is to manually construct query strings instead of using the `params:` keyword argument.

### Workaround

For affected tests, replace:
```ruby
get some_url, params: { id: 123 }, as: :json
```

With:
```ruby
get "#{some_url}?id=123", as: :json
```

### Environment

- Rails version: 8.1.3
- Ruby version: 4.0.2
- Operating system: Ubuntu 25.10

### Related issues

This may be related to or a regression of issues #45409 and #26033. Fix GET+JSON+params in integration tests for API-only apps ### Summary

`params:` in integration test helpers was ambiguous — for GET requests with `as: :json` it was unclear whether params should go in the query string or request body. The original workaround converted GET to POST with `X-Http-Method-Override`, which broke API-only apps that exclude `Rack::MethodOverride`.

### Fix

Add `query:` and `body:` kwargs for explicit control. Remove the POST conversion hack.

- `query:` — always URL query string, any HTTP method
- `body:` — always encoded request body, any HTTP method
- `params:` — unchanged: GET → query string, other → body

```ruby
get  "/search", query: { q: "rails" }, as: :json
post "/search", query: { page: 1 }, body: { filters: {} }, as: :json
```

Fixes #57131

### Benchmark

Ruby 4.0.1, Rails 8.2.0.alpha

| Scenario | main (before) | PR (after) | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| `GET params` (no as:) | 27,428 i/s | 27,178 i/s | same-ish |
| `GET params + as: :json` | 25,946 i/s | 27,667 i/s | **+6.6% faster** |
| `POST params + as: :json` | 28,274 i/s | 27,916 i/s | same-ish |

New kwargs (baseline):

| Scenario | i/s |
|---|---|
| `GET query: + as: :json` | 26,239 |
| `POST body: + as: :json` | 28,126 |
| `POST query:+body: + as: :json` | 23,881 |

issue_title: 7.1 -> 7.2 perf regression due to non permanent ActiveRecord connections
pr_title: Micro-optimize Active Record connection checkin
combined_text:
7.1 -> 7.2 perf regression due to non permanent ActiveRecord connections I observe a ~5% increase in response time since migrating to Rails 7.2.

This regression stems from the changes introduced with `ActiveRecord::Base.with_connection` and `ActiveRecord::Base.lease_connection`.

Indeed, as there's no permanent AR connection anymore, my app now leases then releases it's connection, at least once for **every ActiveRecord query**.

Traced  those `with_connection` calls from `ActiveRecord::Core::ClassMethods#cached_find_by`, `ActiveRecord::Associations::AliasTracker.create`, `AssociationReflection#association_scope_cache`, `ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#find_target`...

This also triggers many, many calls to `checkin` callbacks per request, which is quite wasteful.

I get that the change can be beneficial on busy heavily multi-threaded workers, but I don't see the benefit on single-threaded web workers (`unicorn`) or when the connection pool is large enough for all threads (and when the database able to deal with all those concurrent connections).

Adding a call to `ActiveRecord::Base.lease_connection` as the first `before_action` in the controllers that requires a connection to the DB restores the performance back to Rails 7.1 level.

Is this new default behavior expected?
Should my explicit `lease_connection` hack in my controllers be needed (and does it have a drawback I didn't get?) or did I miss something that would pin the connection at first use?

Thanks!

cc @byroot 

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 7.2+

**Ruby version**: 3.4
 Micro-optimize Active Record connection checkin Ref: https://github.com/rails/rails/issues/55728

Starting in 7.2, since we no longer pin Active Record connection for the entirely of the request or job cycle, `Pool#checkout` and `Pool#checkin` overhead has to be paid for every query. It's not a whole lot once you consider a query will need to do a network hop, but we should still try to keep that overhead low.

This PR contains many different small optimizations, many of them too small to show a clear gain on a benchmark, but together they add up, see individual commits. (FYI: @cmaion).


Which brings up back to about half-way between 7.1 and `main`:

```
ruby 3.4.6 (2025-09-16 revision dbd83256b1) +YJIT +PRISM [arm64-darwin24]
Warming up --------------------------------------
          7-1-stable     3.219k i/100ms
Calculating -------------------------------------
          7-1-stable     88.512k (± 4.1%) i/s   (11.30 μs/i) -    444.222k in   5.027300s

Comparison:
     ar-fast-checkin:    79704.1 i/s
          7-1-stable:    88512.0 i/s - 1.11x  faster
                main:    70279.4 i/s - 1.13x  slower
```

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails", path: "."
  gem "sqlite3"
  gem "benchmark-ips"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "benchmark/ips"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new(nil)
  config.log_level = :info
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
end
Rails.application.initialize!


ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :posts, force: true do |t|
  end

  create_table :comments, force: true do |t|
    t.integer :post_id
  end
end

ActiveRecord::Base.release_connection unless Rails::VERSION::STRING < "7.2"

class Post < ActiveRecord::Base
end

BRANCH = `git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD`.strip

Benchmark.ips do |x|
  x.report(BRANCH) { Post.count }
  x.save!("/tmp/bench-ar-data")
  x.compare!(order: :baseline)
end
```

There might be some more optimizations to perform, but I think this is now large enough to be worth merging.

issue_title: Regexp in `ActiveRecord::LogSubscriber` times out on extremely long queries, preventing them from being logged
pr_title: Speedup ActiveRecord::LogSubscriber#sql_color
combined_text:
Regexp in `ActiveRecord::LogSubscriber` times out on extremely long queries, preventing them from being logged ### Steps to reproduce

When a query contains many `select ` and is extremely long, [regexp](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/706b9daab37664710de20cca273eb9ffa5361ec3/activerecord/lib/active_record/log_subscriber.rb#L87) evaluation inside `ActiveRecord::LogSubscriber#sql_color` takes an excessive amount of time.

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"
end

require "active_record"

very_long_body = "select #{'a' * 1000}" * 100_000
puts "bytesize: #{very_long_body.bytesize / 1024 / 1024} MB"

started_at = Time.now
ActiveRecord::LogSubscriber.allocate.send(:sql_color, very_long_body)
puts "elapsed: #{Time.now - started_at}"
```

Result:

```
$ ruby benchmark.rb
...snip...
bytesize: 96 MB
elapsed: 3.320321
```

Since Rails sets `Regexp.timeout = 1` by [default](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/706b9daab37664710de20cca273eb9ffa5361ec3/railties/lib/rails/application/configuration.rb#L344), this results in timeout error during instrumentation.
When this timeout occurs, the query is not logged at all, and instead, the following error is logged:

```
Could not log "sql.active_record" event. Regexp::TimeoutError: regexp match timeout
```

The example code is artificial; however, we have use cases where very long texts or binaries are bulk-inserted, causing the regexp to time out and making those queries invisible in the logs.

### Expected behavior

The query should be logged even if it's too large to be colorized efficiently.
LogSubscriber should rescue `Regexp::TimeoutError` and log the original uncolorized string as a fallback.

### Actual behavior

The query is lost. Only the following error is recorded:

```
Could not log "sql.active_record" event. Regexp::TimeoutError: regexp match timeout
```

This makes it difficult to debug issues involving large payloads.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.3

**Ruby version**: 4.0.1
 Speedup ActiveRecord::LogSubscriber#sql_color Fix: https://github.com/rails/rails/issues/57092
Close: https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/57102

SQL queries can be very long, hence unanchored regexp, even if they have linear performance, may take longer than Regexp.timeout.

issue_title: ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#inversable? does not work with composite keys
pr_title: Fix inverse_of on composite keys in ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#inversable?
combined_text:
ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#inversable? does not work with composite keys ### Steps to reproduce

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  # Activate the gem you are reporting the issue against.
  gem "activerecord", "~> 8.1" # Also happens on 7.2 (didn't test earlier as Trilogy wasn't available)
  gem "trilogy"
  gem "debug"
end

require "active_record"
require "logger"

module ActiveRecordCompositeKeyInversePatch
  private

  def matches_foreign_key?(record)
    fk = reflection.foreign_key

    unless fk.is_a?(Array)
      return super
    end

    pk = Array(reflection.association_primary_key)

    owner_fk_values = fk.map { |key| owner._read_attribute(key) }
    record_pk_values = pk.map { |key| record._read_attribute(key) }

    owner_fk_values == record_pk_values
  end
end

# Patching with the above module fixes the issue as composite keys
# ActiveRecord::Associations::Association.prepend(ActiveRecordCompositeKeyInversePatch)

logger = Logger.new(STDOUT)
ActiveRecord::Base.logger = logger

ActiveRecord::Base.establish_connection(
  adapter: "trilogy",
  database: "test",
  host: ENV.fetch("MYSQL_HOST"),
  port: ENV.fetch("MYSQL_PORT"),
  password: ENV.fetch("MYSQL_PASSWORD"),
  username: ENV.fetch("MYSQL_USER")
)

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :parents, force: true, primary_key: [:store_id, :id] do |t|
    t.integer :store_id, null: false
    t.integer :id, null: false
    t.string :last_name
  end

  create_table :children, force: true do |t|
    t.integer :store_id, null: false
    t.integer :parent_id, null: false
  end
end

class Parent < ActiveRecord::Base
  self.primary_key = :id

  has_many :children, inverse_of: :parent, foreign_key: :parent_id, primary_key: :id

  query_constraints :store_id, :id

  validates :id, presence: true
  validates :store_id, presence: true
end

class Child < ActiveRecord::Base
  belongs_to :parent, inverse_of: :children, foreign_key: [:store_id, :parent_id], primary_key: [:store_id, :id]

  validates :parent_id, presence: true
  validates :store_id, presence: true
end

parent = Parent.new
parent.store_id = 1
parent.id = 1
parent.last_name = "Smith"
parent.save!
parent.reload
logger.info("Parent: #{parent.to_json}")

child = Child.new
child.store_id = parent.store_id
child.parent_id = parent.id
child.save!

child = Child.first!
logger.info("Parent object_id: #{child.parent.object_id}")
logger.info("Navigation parent object_id: #{child.parent.children.first.parent.object_id}")
unless child.parent.object_id == child.parent.children.first.parent.object_id
  logger.error "inverse_of isn't set on child.parent.children.first.parent"
end

```

### Expected behavior

The `ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#inversable?` method works with composite keys, resulting in re-using the already loaded object instance instead of doing a new query and instantiating a new object.

The following queries would be executed:
```
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.505020 #53610] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.2ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.507674 #53610] DEBUG -- :   Parent Load (0.4ms)  SELECT `parents`.* FROM `parents` WHERE `parents`.`store_id` = 1 AND `parents`.`id` = 1 LIMIT 1
I, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.507980 #53610]  INFO -- : Parent object_id: 1960
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.509779 #53610] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.3ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` WHERE `children`.`parent_id` = 1 ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
I, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.509846 #53610]  INFO -- : Navigation parent object_id: 1960
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.510247 #53610] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.3ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` WHERE `children`.`parent_id` = 1 ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
```

### Actual behavior

The `ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#inversable?` method calls `matches_foreign_key?(record)`, but `matches_foreign_key?(record)` doesn't take into account composite keys:

```ruby
        def inversable?(record)
          record &&
            ((!record.persisted? || !owner.persisted?) || matches_foreign_key?(record))
        end

        def matches_foreign_key?(record)
          if foreign_key_for?(record)
            record.read_attribute(reflection.foreign_key) == owner.id ||
              (foreign_key_for?(owner) && owner.read_attribute(reflection.foreign_key) == record.id)
          else
            owner.read_attribute(reflection.foreign_key) == record.id
          end
        end
```

`record.read_attribute` ends up receiving `["store_id", "parent_id"]` which doesn't exist and returns `nil`:
```ruby
(rdbg) fk
=> ["store_id", "parent_id"]
(ruby) record.read_attribute(fk)
=> nil
```


ActiveRecord fails to determine the relationship is `inversable?`, and ends up triggering a new query for the Parent, and loads a new instance:
```
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.173841 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.2ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.177039 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Parent Load (0.4ms)  SELECT `parents`.* FROM `parents` WHERE `parents`.`store_id` = 1 AND `parents`.`id` = 1 LIMIT 1
I, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.177438 #53687]  INFO -- : Parent object_id: 1960
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.179559 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.2ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` WHERE `children`.`parent_id` = 1 ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.180296 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Parent Load (0.2ms)  SELECT `parents`.* FROM `parents` WHERE `parents`.`store_id` = 1 AND `parents`.`id` = 1 LIMIT 1
I, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.180342 #53687]  INFO -- : Navigation parent object_id: 1968
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.180618 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.1ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` WHERE `children`.`parent_id` = 1 ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.180886 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Parent Load (0.1ms)  SELECT `parents`.* FROM `parents` WHERE `parents`.`store_id` = 1 AND `parents`.`id` = 1 LIMIT 1
E, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.180913 #53687] ERROR -- : inverse_of isn't set on child.parent.children.first.parent
```

Interestingly, `ActiveRecord::Associations::Preloader.new(records: [parent], associations: [:children]).call` makes it work properly.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 7 and 8 (latest in rubygems)

**Ruby version**:  3.4.7
 Fix inverse_of on composite keys in ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#inversable?  ### Motivation / Background

Fixes https://github.com/rails/rails/issues/56662

The issue posted a simple replication script, including a monkeypatch that would address the issue. This PR refactors that patch into an integral part of the `ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#matches_foreign_key?` method.

### Detail

Composite keys would fail on the `record.read_attribute` and `record._has_attribute?` checks as this would pass in an array to a method expecting a string.

Each key component needs to be read one-by-one.

### Checklist

Before submitting the PR make sure the following are checked:

* [x] This Pull Request is related to one change. Unrelated changes should be opened in separate PRs.
* [x] Commit message has a detailed description of what changed and why. If this PR fixes a related issue include it in the commit message. Ex: `[Fix #issue-number]`
* [x] Tests are added or updated if you fix a bug or add a feature.
* [x] CHANGELOG files are updated for the changed libraries if there is a behavior change or additional feature. Minor bug fixes and documentation changes should not be included.

issue_title: ActiveStorage 8.1.3: NameError uninitialized constant ActiveStorage::OpenSSL
pr_title: Add missing require openssl to ActiveStorage
combined_text:
ActiveStorage 8.1.3: NameError uninitialized constant ActiveStorage::OpenSSL ### Steps to reproduce

1. Create a minimal Rails app or test file that requires `active_storage` directly:

```ruby
require "active_storage"
```

2. Run the file

### Expected behavior

Active Storage loads successfully.

### Actual behavior

```
NameError: uninitialized constant ActiveStorage::OpenSSL
```

### Root cause

PR #54040 (commit e64e5d31) added `checksum_implementation` to support FIPS compliance. The code in `lib/active_storage.rb` references `OpenSSL::Digest::MD5` at module load time (line 368) but never requires `openssl`:

```ruby
singleton_class.attr_accessor :checksum_implementation
@checksum_implementation = OpenSSL::Digest::MD5
begin
  @checksum_implementation.hexdigest("test")
rescue # OpenSSL may have MD5 disabled
  require "digest/md5"
  @checksum_implementation = Digest::MD5
end
```

This works in most Rails apps because something else (credentials, message verifier, etc.) typically requires `openssl` before Active Storage loads. But when Active Storage is required in isolation, it fails.

### Suggested fix

Add `require "openssl"` to `lib/active_storage.rb` before the `checksum_implementation` code.

### System configuration

- **Rails version**: 8.1.3
- **Ruby version**: 3.4.8 Add missing require openssl to ActiveStorage Fixes #57098.

issue_title: `create_table` raises ArgumentError for valid table names when schema-prefixed (PostgreSQL multiple schemas)
pr_title: Ignore schemas when validating table name lengths for PostgreSQL
combined_text:
`create_table` raises ArgumentError for valid table names when schema-prefixed (PostgreSQL multiple schemas) ## Summary

When a Rails application is configured with multiple PostgreSQL schemas via
`schema_search_path`, the schema dumper writes table names qualified with their
schema into `schema.rb`:

```ruby
create_table "public.my_table_name", force: :cascade do |t|
```

When Rails loads this schema back (`db:schema:load`, `db:test:prepare`), the
`validate_table_length!` check introduced in Rails 7.1 measures the length of
the full `"public.my_table_name"` string instead of just the bare identifier
`"my_table_name"`. This causes valid table names to be rejected with an
`ArgumentError`, making `db:test:prepare` permanently broken for apps that use
multiple schemas and have table names near the length limit.

## Steps to reproduce

Run the attached reproduction script against any PostgreSQL database:

```bash
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:pass@localhost/mydb ruby rails_schema_prefix_length_bug.rb
```

The script demonstrates:

1. A 52-char table name is created without issue (52 ≤ 58-char limit).
2. The same name prefixed with `public.` (59 chars total) raises `ArgumentError`
   when passed to `create_table` — exactly as the schema dumper would write it.
3. PostgreSQL itself accepts the schema-prefixed DDL via raw SQL with no error,
   proving the problem is in Rails' validation alone.

<details>
<summary>Reproduction script</summary>

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true
require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"
  gem "activerecord", "~> 7.1"
  gem "pg"
  gem "minitest"
end

require "active_record"
require "minitest/autorun"

ActiveRecord::Base.establish_connection(
  ENV["DATABASE_URL"] || {
    adapter:  "postgresql",
    database: ENV.fetch("PGDATABASE", "rails_bug_repro"),
    host:     ENV.fetch("PGHOST",     "localhost"),
    username: ENV.fetch("PGUSER",     nil),
    password: ENV.fetch("PGPASSWORD", nil),
  }
)

# PostgreSQL limit = max_identifier_length(63) - "_pkey"(5) = 58 chars.
# 52-char name passes bare (52 <= 58) but fails schema-prefixed (59 > 58).
BARE_TABLE_NAME     = "t" * 52
PREFIXED_TABLE_NAME = "public.#{BARE_TABLE_NAME}"

class SchemaPrefixTableNameLengthBugTest < Minitest::Test
  def setup
    connection.execute("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS #{PREFIXED_TABLE_NAME}")
    connection.drop_table(BARE_TABLE_NAME, if_exists: true)
  end

  def teardown
    connection.execute("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS #{PREFIXED_TABLE_NAME}")
    connection.drop_table(BARE_TABLE_NAME, if_exists: true)
  end

  def test_bare_table_name_within_limit_succeeds
    connection.create_table(BARE_TABLE_NAME, force: true)
    assert connection.table_exists?(BARE_TABLE_NAME)
  end

  def test_schema_prefixed_table_name_incorrectly_raises
    error = assert_raises(ArgumentError) do
      connection.create_table(PREFIXED_TABLE_NAME, force: true)
    end
    assert_match(/too long/i, error.message)
    puts "\n[BUG] #{error.message}"
    puts "  Bare identifier : #{BARE_TABLE_NAME.length} chars (limit #{connection.table_name_length})"
    puts "  String checked  : #{PREFIXED_TABLE_NAME.length} chars (\"#{PREFIXED_TABLE_NAME}\")"
  end

  def test_postgresql_accepts_schema_prefixed_table_via_raw_sql
    connection.execute("CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS #{PREFIXED_TABLE_NAME} (id bigserial PRIMARY KEY)")
    result = connection.execute(
      "SELECT tablename FROM pg_tables WHERE schemaname='public' AND tablename='#{BARE_TABLE_NAME}'"
    )
    assert_equal 1, result.ntuples
    puts "\n[CONTROL] PostgreSQL created #{PREFIXED_TABLE_NAME} without errors"
  end

  private
  def connection = ActiveRecord::Base.connection
end
```

</details>

## Expected behavior

`create_table "public.my_table_name"` should succeed when the bare identifier
`my_table_name` is within the PostgreSQL identifier length limit. The schema
qualifier is not part of the identifier that PostgreSQL enforces the limit on.

## Actual behavior

```
ArgumentError: Table name 'public.tttt...t' is too long (59 characters); the limit is 58 characters
```

The error is raised even though the actual table identifier (`"t" * 52`) is
only 52 characters — well within the 58-character limit.

## System configuration

- **Rails version:** 7.1.0+ (introduced in commit `1ec879d02b`)
- **Ruby version:** any
- **Database:** PostgreSQL (any version)
- **Adapter:** `pg`

Not affected: Rails 7.0.x and below (`validate_table_length!` did not exist). Ignore schemas when validating table name lengths for PostgreSQL Fixes #57093.

issue_title: Parallelization::Server#shutdown hangs when workers die after initial WNOHANG sweep (#55794 gap)
pr_title: Fix parallel test shutdown hang when workers die during Server#shutdown
combined_text:
Parallelization::Server#shutdown hangs when workers die after initial WNOHANG sweep (#55794 gap) ### Steps to reproduce

#55794 (fixing #55513) added a `Process.waitpid(pid, WNOHANG)` sweep in `Parallelization#shutdown` to detect dead workers **before** calling `Server#shutdown`. This helps when workers are already dead at shutdown time, but doesn't cover the case where workers exit **during** `Server#shutdown`'s `while active_workers?` loop.

If a worker finishes its tests and exits without calling `stop_worker` over DRb (e.g. a `parallelize_teardown` hook raises, the DRb connection drops, or the worker is OOM-killed mid-cleanup), and this happens **after** the initial WNOHANG sweep, `Server#shutdown` loops forever.

We're hitting this intermittently in CI with system tests (Capybara/Cuprite). Workers become zombies, but the parent hangs until a watchdog kills it ~90s later.

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails", "~> 8.1.0"
  # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
  # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"
end

require "active_support"
require "active_support/testing/parallelization"
require "minitest/autorun"

class ParallelShutdownBugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  # Demonstrates that a worker exiting without calling stop_worker
  # AFTER Parallelization#shutdown's initial WNOHANG sweep causes
  # Server#shutdown to loop forever on `while active_workers?`.
  #
  # This is the gap left by rails/rails#55794 which only sweeps once
  # before entering Server#shutdown.
  def test_shutdown_detects_workers_that_die_during_server_shutdown
    parallelization = ActiveSupport::Testing::Parallelization.new(1)

    # Access internals to set up the scenario
    server = parallelization.instance_variable_get(:@queue_server)
    url = parallelization.instance_variable_get(:@url)

    # Push one unit of work so the worker has something to do
    server << ["BugTest", "test_stuff", Minitest::CompositeReporter.new]

    # Fork a worker that registers, does work, but exits without
    # calling stop_worker (simulates cleanup hook raising or DRb failure)
    worker_pid = fork do
      DRb.stop_service
      queue = DRbObject.new_with_uri(url)
      queue.start_worker(0, Process.pid)
      queue.pop
      sleep 0.3 # simulate test execution
      exit!(0)  # exit without stop_worker
    end

    # Small delay so worker registers but hasn't exited yet
    sleep 0.1

    # This is Parallelization#shutdown (with #55794 fix):
    # 1. WNOHANG sweep — worker is still alive, finds nothing
    dead_worker_pids = [worker_pid].filter_map do |pid|
      Process.waitpid(pid, Process::WNOHANG)
    rescue Errno::ECHILD
      pid
    end
    server.remove_dead_workers(dead_worker_pids)

    assert_equal 0, dead_worker_pids.size, "Worker should still be alive during sweep"

    # 2. Server#shutdown — worker exits during this wait, hangs forever
    assert_raises(Timeout::Error) do
      Timeout.timeout(3) { server.shutdown }
    end

    # Clean up zombie
    Process.waitpid(worker_pid, Process::WNOHANG) rescue nil
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

`Server#shutdown` should detect workers that die during its `while active_workers?` loop and exit cleanly.

A fix would be to move dead-worker detection **into** the loop:

```ruby
def shutdown
  sleep 0.1 until @queue.empty?
  @queue.close

  while active_workers?
    dead_pids = @worker_pids.each_value.select do |pid|
      Process.waitpid(pid, Process::WNOHANG)
    rescue Errno::ECHILD
      true
    end
    remove_dead_workers(dead_pids) if dead_pids.any?
    sleep 0.1
  end

  # ... report in-flight tests
end
```

### Actual behavior

`Server#shutdown` loops forever on `while active_workers?`. The worker is a zombie (exited), but the server never checks whether worker PIDs are still alive inside the loop.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1.2 (also affects 8.0.4 and any version with #55794 applied)

**Ruby version**: 3.3.10 Fix parallel test shutdown hang when workers die during Server#shutdown ### Motivation / Background

#55794 (fixing #55513) added a `Process.waitpid(pid, WNOHANG)` sweep in `Parallelization#shutdown` to detect dead workers **before** calling `Server#shutdown`. This helps when workers are already dead at shutdown time, but doesn't cover the case where workers exit **during** `Server#shutdown`'s `wait_for_active_workers` loop.

If a worker finishes its tests and exits without calling `stop_worker` over DRb (e.g. a `parallelize_teardown` hook raises, the DRb connection drops, or the worker is OOM-killed mid-cleanup), and this happens **after** the initial WNOHANG sweep, `Server#shutdown` loops forever on `while active_workers?`.

We're hitting this intermittently in CI with system tests (Capybara/Cuprite). Workers become zombies, but the parent hangs until a watchdog kills it ~90s later.

Fixes #57052

### Detail

Adds a `reap_dead_workers` method to `Server` that uses `Process.waitpid(pid, WNOHANG)` to detect exited worker processes, and calls it inside the `wait_for_active_workers` loop. This catches workers that die at any point during shutdown — before or during the wait.

### Additional information

A regression test is included that forks a worker, lets it register, then kills it *after* the parent enters `Server#shutdown`. Without the fix, the test hangs; with it, shutdown completes cleanly.

issue_title: `validates` per-validator options replace top-level options
pr_title: Combine per-validator and top-level :if/:unless/:on in validates
combined_text:
`validates` per-validator options replace top-level options ### Steps to reproduce

<!-- (Guidelines for creating a bug report are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#creating-a-bug-report)) -->

<!-- Paste your executable test case created from one of the scripts found [here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#create-an-executable-test-case) below: -->
```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"
  # If you want to test against edge Rails replace the previous line with this:
  # gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"

  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :posts, force: true do |t|
    t.string :title
    t.boolean :local
    t.boolean :global
  end
end

class Post < ActiveRecord::Base
  validates :title, presence: { if: :local? }, if: :global?
end

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_association_stuff
    assert_not Post.new(local: true, global: true).valid?

    assert Post.new(local: false, global: true).valid?
    assert Post.new(local: false, global: false).valid?

    # Presence's :if (local) replaces validates's :if (global), so whether global? is truthy or falsey is irrelevant
    assert Post.new(local: true, global: false).valid?
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

I expected the validation to only be enforced when both `local` and `global` were truthy.

### Actual behavior

The validation is enforced when `local` is truthy, regardless of the value of `global`.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: main

**Ruby version**: 3.4.5

### More Info

That's an over-simplified reproduction.

More specifically, I have a number of validations I want to run only when a certain condition passes, then within those validations, a subset have an additional criteria.

I tried something like:
```ruby
with_options(if: :global?) do |o|
  o.validates :title, presence: true, if: :local?
  o.validates :author, presence: true
  # and so on
end
```

hoping it'd become something like (not literally expecting Rails to define a method combining the conditions):
```ruby
validates :title, presence: true, if: :local_and_global?
validates :author, presence: true, if: :global?
```

But `with_options` documents that the inner options take precedence over the options given to `with_options`, so that wouldn't work the way I wanted, as that becomes:
```ruby
validates :title, presence: true, if: :local?
validates :author, presence: true, if: :global?
```

Then I considered moving the condition inside the specific validator (`presence`), rather than at the `validates` level, along the lines of:
```ruby
with_options(if: :global?) do |o|
  o.validates :title, presence: { if: :local? }
  o.validates :author, presence: true
  # and so on
end
```
hoping it would effectively combine the `:if`s, checking both, yielding something equivalent to:
```ruby
validates :title, presence: { if: :local? }, if: :global?
validates :author, presence: true, if: :global?
```

It seems the options given to individual validators take precedence over the options given at the `validates` level, so that really just evaluates to:
```ruby
validates_presence_of :title, if: :local?
```

Best I can tell, having a validator-specific `:if` and `validates`-level `:if` is not explicitly [documented](https://api.rubyonrails.org/classes/ActiveModel/Validations/ClassMethods.html#method-i-validates), nor is it explicitly [tested](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/a821b31a40a03404412a1aafeb9c3190f138ac0b/activemodel/test/cases/validations/validates_test.rb), leading me to believe this combination is simply undefined as to what the expected behavior should be.

I could see three directions to take this in:
1. document that when providing the same option at both levels, the inner one wins
2. check both conditions to enforce validation (though this could be breaking for applications reliant on the current behavior). Perhaps this could be accomplished by adding array support to `:if` where all conditions need to pass to enforce the validation, then Rails could combine both levels of `:if`s into an array automatically (and the end-user can use an array where needed, separately from this issue).
3. disallow/error when the same option is provided at both levels, as the intended effects could be ambiguous (replace or combine?)

There's likely a broader question here in that whatever is decided for `:if` would likely apply to `:unless`, but would it also apply to the other options (ie. `:on`, `:except_on`, `:allow_nil`, `:allow_blank`, or `:strict`)? Combine per-validator and top-level :if/:unless/:on in validates ### Motivation / Background

Fixes #55761.

When using `validates` with both top-level and per-validator `:if`/`:unless`/`:on` options, the per-validator options silently replace the top-level ones instead of combining them:

```ruby
validates :title, presence: { if: :local? }, if: :global?
# Expected: validation runs when BOTH local? AND global? are true
# Actual:   validation runs when local? is true (global? is ignored)
```

This happens because `validates` uses `Hash#merge` which replaces duplicate keys.

### Detail

~~This PR contains two commits:~~

~~**1. Optimize callback condition evaluation (`activesupport`)**~~

~~Compiles callback conditions into a single callable instead of iterating an array with `.all?` on every invocation:~~
~~- 0 conditions → `nil` (no-op check)~~
~~- 1 condition → the lambda itself (direct call, no array iteration)~~
~~- 2 conditions → `first.call && second.call` (direct `&&`, no `all?`)~~
~~- 3+ conditions → `conditions.all? { ... }` (existing behavior)~~

~~This speeds up **all** callbacks in Rails (validations, lifecycle hooks, controller filters).~~

**2. Combine per-validator and top-level options (`activemodel`)**

Adds `_merge_validates_options` that combines `:if`, `:unless`, and `:on` into arrays when specified at both levels. Scalar options (`:message`, `:allow_nil`, `:allow_blank`, `:strict`) keep the existing override behavior.

```ruby
validates :title, presence: { if: :local? }, if: :global?
# Now equivalent to:
validates_presence_of :title, if: [:global?, :local?]
```

### Benchmark Results

Validation execution (Ruby 4.0.1):

| Scenario | Before (main) | After | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| top-level `:if` only | 424,777 i/s | **434,393 i/s** | **+2.3% faster** |
| per-validator `:if` only | 422,107 i/s | **427,205 i/s** | **+1.2% faster** |
| combined `:if` (both levels) | 424,330 i/s | 410,180 i/s | -3.3% |
| multi-validator combined | 206,672 i/s | 203,175 i/s | -1.7% |
| class definition | 5,368 i/s | 5,430 i/s | same-ish |

The single-condition paths (the common case) are **faster** than before thanks to the callbacks optimization. The combined case is ~3% slower, which is the irreducible cost of evaluating 2 conditions instead of 1.

### Checklist

- [x] Tests added for `:if`, `:unless`, `:on` combining and scalar override preservation
- [x] Full ActiveSupport test suite passes (5,849 runs, 0 failures)
- [x] Full ActiveModel test suite passes (1,146 runs, 0 failures)
- [x] RuboCop clean
- [x] RDoc updated on `validates` method
- [x] CHANGELOG entry added
- [x] Benchmarked before and after

Thanks for contributing to Rails!

Please do not make *Draft* pull requests, as they still send
notifications to everyone watching the Rails repo.

Create a pull request when it is ready for review and feedback
from the Rails team :).

If your pull request affects documentation or any non-code
changes, guidelines for those changes are [available
here](https://edgeguides.rubyonrails.org/contributing_to_ruby_on_rails.html#contributing-to-the-rails-documentation)

About this template

The following template aims to help contributors write a good description for their pull requests.
We'd like you to provide a description of the changes in your pull request (i.e. bugs fixed or features added), the motivation behind the changes, and complete the checklist below before opening a pull request.

Feel free to discard it if you need to (e.g. when you just fix a typo). -->

### Motivation / Background

Fixes: #56812 

The `LeakChecker` module, which is `prepend`ed onto `ActiveSupport::TestCase`, detects environment variable leaks between tests. After its introduction, four tests in `PostgresqlDbConsoleTest` started failing because the ENV cleanup was happening **after** `LeakChecker` had already inspected the environment:

- `test_postgresql_full`
- `test_postgresql_with_ssl`
- `test_postgresql_include_password`
- `test_postgresql_include_variables`

The failures look like:

```
Environment leak detected:
  - Added variables:
    - PGPORT
  - Changed variables:
    - PGUSER from "postgres" to "user"
    - PGHOST from "postgres" to "host"
```

### Detail

This Pull Request changes the ENV variable backup/restore strategy in `PostgresqlDbConsoleTest` from a `#run` override to a `setup`/`teardown` pair.

The previous approach overrode `#run` and wrapped `super` with a `preserve_pg_env` helper that used an `ensure` block to restore ENV vars. The problem is that `LeakChecker#after_teardown` runs **inside** the `super` call, so it checks ENV before the `ensure` block has a chance to restore it:

```
#run -> preserve_pg_env do
  super -> before_setup (LeakChecker snapshots ENV)
        -> test body (sets PGUSER, PGHOST, etc.)
        -> after_teardown (LeakChecker detects leak!) ❌
ensure
  restore ENV (too late)
```

The fix uses `setup`/`teardown`, which are part of the Minitest lifecycle that runs **before** `LeakChecker#after_teardown`:

```
before_setup (LeakChecker snapshots ENV)
setup (backs up PG env vars)
test body (sets PGUSER, PGHOST, etc.)
teardown (restores PG env vars) ✅
after_teardown (LeakChecker finds no diff) ✅
```

The now-unnecessary `preserve_pg_env` private method has been removed.

### Additional information

- Only the test file is modified; no production code changes.
- The `LeakChecker` module is defined in `tools/support/leak_checker.rb` and prepended in `tools/test_common.rb`.

### Checklist

Before submitting the PR make sure the following are checked:

* [x] Commit message has a detailed description of what changed and why. If this PR fixes a related issue include it in the commit message. Ex: `[Fix #issue-number]`
* [x] Tests are added or updated if you fix a bug or add a feature.
* [ ] CHANGELOG files are updated for the changed libraries if there is a behavior change or additional feature. Minor bug fixes and documentation changes should not be included.

jemalloc still recommended? Not really a bug, but the guides mention to open an issue when something is wrong, so hope this is ok.

### Problem

The guide [Tuning Performance for Deployment](https://guides.rubyonrails.org/tuning_performance_for_deployment.html) refers to [jemalloc](https://github.com/jemalloc/jemalloc) for memory optimization, but its repository was archived on 2025-06-02.
The alternative way is described as "less efficient alternative", which brings up the question if it's still the correct way to use `jemalloc` or something alternative is suggested from now on?

### Expected behavior

The guide is updated according to new suggestion.

### References
- https://jasone.github.io/2025/06/12/jemalloc-postmortem/ Fix link to jemalloc repo [ci skip]  ### Motivation / Background
