issue_title: SQLite virtual tables do not respect `ignore_tables`
pr_title: Fix SQLite virtual tables not ignored by `ignore_tables`
combined_text:
SQLite virtual tables do not respect `ignore_tables` ### Steps to reproduce

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  gem "rails"
  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"

ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"

  config.active_record.schema_format = :ruby
end
Rails.application.initialize!

ActiveRecord::SchemaDumper.ignore_tables << "fts_docs"

connection = ActiveRecord::Base.connection
connection.execute <<~SQL
  CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE fts_docs USING fts5(content)
SQL

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_ignore_tables_applies_to_virtual_tables_in_schema_dump
    stream = StringIO.new
    ActiveRecord::SchemaDumper.dump(ActiveRecord::Base.connection_pool, stream)
    dump = stream.string

    assert_not_includes dump, 'create_virtual_table "fts_docs"'
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

Schema dump should respect `ActiveRecord::SchemaDumper.ignore_tables` for SQLite virtual tables and omit ignored virtual tables from `schema.rb` output. This is relevant for generated virtual tables (e.g. Spatialite, see #56969).

### Actual behavior

Ignored virtual tables are still emitted in the schema dump. For the script above, `fts_docs` is still included as:

```ruby
create_virtual_table "fts_docs", "fts5", ["content"]
```

#### Proposed Fix

Check if virtual tables are ignored. 

```ruby
# activerecord/lib/active_record/connection_adapters/sqlite3/schema_dumper.rb
def virtual_tables(stream)
  virtual_tables = @connection.virtual_tables.reject { |name, _| ignored?(name) }
  # ....
end
```

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1

**Ruby version**: 4.0.1, but shouldn't matter
 Fix SQLite virtual tables not ignored by `ignore_tables` ### Motivation / Background

Fixes #56976

### Detail

See related issue for relevant details. This enables virtual tables to be ignored using `ActiveRecord::SchemaDumper.ignore_tables`

issue_title: SQLite virtual tables without parenthesis produces broken `schema.rb`
pr_title: Fix parsing SQLite virtual tables without parenthesis
combined_text:
SQLite virtual tables without parenthesis produces broken `schema.rb` ### Steps to reproduce

This bug occurs when using [SpatiaLite](https://www.gaia-gis.it/fossil/libspatialite/index) with GpkgMode & spatial metadata. Note that you must have Spatialite installed on your system so the extension is available. 

```bash
# Debian / Ubuntu / Linux Mint
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y libsqlite3-mod-spatialite

# Fedora / RHEL / CentOS / Rocky Linux / AlmaLinux
sudo dnf install -y libspatialite

# Arch Linux / Manjaro
sudo pacman -S libspatialite
 ```

```ruby
require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"
  gem "rails"
  gem "sqlite3"
end

require "active_record/railtie"
require "minitest/autorun"
require "stringio"

# This connection will do for database-independent bug reports.
ENV["DATABASE_URL"] = "sqlite3::memory:"

class TestApp < Rails::Application
  config.load_defaults Rails::VERSION::STRING.to_f
  config.eager_load = false
  config.logger = Logger.new($stdout)
  config.secret_key_base = "secret_key_base"

  config.active_record.encryption.primary_key = "primary_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.deterministic_key = "deterministic_key"
  config.active_record.encryption.key_derivation_salt = "key_derivation_salt"
end
Rails.application.initialize!

# Configure SQLite with SpatiaLite extension
ActiveRecord::Base.establish_connection(
  adapter: "sqlite3",
  database: ":memory:",
  extensions: ["mod_spatialite"]
)

# Initialize SpatiaLite (creates virtual tables with empty parentheses)
connection = ActiveRecord::Base.connection
connection.execute("SELECT EnableGpkgMode();")
connection.execute("SELECT InitSpatialMetadata(1);")

class BugTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  def test_schema_dump_with_spatialite_virtual_tables
    output = StringIO.new
    ActiveRecord::SchemaDumper.dump(ActiveRecord::Base.connection.pool, output)
  end
end
```

### Expected behavior

Schema dump should produce valid Ruby code and not result in an invalid `schema.rb`

### Actual behavior

Running `bin/rails db:migrate` with these virtual tables crashes the schema dumper, which  produces a broken `db/schema.rb` missing its closing `end`. That's not valid Ruby code (duh) and causes all kinds of follow-up problems. The user has to go in and manually fix the `schema.rb` (by adding the missing end) after each migration.

#### Root Cause

SpatiaLite registers three virtual tables with **empty parentheses**:

```sql
CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE SpatialIndex USING VirtualSpatialIndex()
CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE ElementaryGeometries USING VirtualElementary()
CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE KNN2 USING VirtualKNN2()
```

Rails 8.1 added virtual table support for SQLite (primarily for FTS5) and parses them with:

```ruby
# activerecord/lib/active_record/connection_adapters/sqlite3_adapter.rb:348
VIRTUAL_TABLE_REGEX = /USING\s+(\w+)\s*\((.+)\)/i
```

The `.+` requires **at least one character** inside the parentheses. SpatiaLite's tables have empty parens `()`, so the regex doesn't match. `.match()` returns `nil`, `.to_a` gives `[]`, and both `module_name` and `arguments` end up `nil`, which causes the crash. 

#### Proposed Fix

The fix is trivial. Change the Regex that parses Virtual tables to match empty parenthesis.

```ruby
VIRTUAL_TABLE_REGEX = /USING\s+(\w+)\s*\((.*)\)/i
```

The new regex `(.*)` matches zero or more characters.  Matching  `()` returns empty string `""`, which is fine because  `"".split(", ")` returns `[]` without error. 

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.1

**Ruby version**:  Tested with `4.0.1` but should apply to any
 Fix parsing SQLite virtual tables without parenthesis Fixes #56969

issue_title: Undocumented behavior change in `ActionController::Parameters#transform_keys!` (Rails 7.2 vs 8.0.3)
pr_title: [ci skip] Update CHANGELOG with transform_keys! overwrite precedence note
combined_text:
Undocumented behavior change in `ActionController::Parameters#transform_keys!` (Rails 7.2 vs 8.0.3) ### Steps to reproduce

You can run the script below. By changing `rails_version`, you can see the test passes in 7.2.0 but fails in 8.0.3.

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

# rails_version = "7.2.0"
rails_version = "8.0.3"

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"
  gem "rails", rails_version
end

require "minitest/autorun"
require "action_controller"

class ParametersTransformKeysTest < Minitest::Test
  def test_transform_keys_behavior_change
    params = ActionController::Parameters.new(xxYy: { a: 1 }, xx_yy: {})
    params.transform_keys!(&:underscore)

    # In Rails 7.2, the camel case key's value was preserved.
    # In Rails 8.0.3, this test fails because the snake case key overwrites the camel case one (returns {"xx_yy"=>{}}).
    assert_equal({"xx_yy"=>{"a"=>1}}, params.to_unsafe_h)
  end
end

```

### Expected behavior

In Rails 7.2, when transforming keys causes a key collision, the earlier key's value was preserved. The script above passes in `7.2.0`.

### Actual behavior

In Rails 8.0.3, the later key now correctly overwrites the earlier key (matching standard Ruby `Hash` behavior), causing the script above to fail.

While the new behavior in 8.0.3 seems correct, it is an undocumented breaking change for existing applications. I suspect this is an intentional bug fix (perhaps related to the recent fixes for `ActiveSupport::HashWithIndifferentAccess#transform_keys!` in v8.0.3).

It would be highly appreciated if this change could be explicitly documented in the Changelog or Release Notes to prevent confusion during upgrades.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 8.0.3 (changed from 7.2)

**Ruby version**: 3.3.7

### Motivation / Background

<!--
Describe why this Pull Request needs to be merged. What bug have you fixed? What feature have you added? Why is it important?
If you are fixing a specific issue, include "Fixes #ISSUE" (replace with the issue number, remove the quotes) and the issue will be linked to this PR.
-->

This Pull Request has been created because the previous bug fix for `ActiveSupport::HashWithIndifferentAccess#transform_keys!` in Rails 8.0.3 introduced a change in overwrite precedence that was not explicitly documented. 

When distinct keys are transformed into the exact same key, the later key now correctly overwrites the earlier one (matching standard Ruby `Hash` behavior). Previously, the earlier key's value was preserved. Documenting this is important to prevent unexpected data loss or silent bugs for users upgrading existing applications.

Fixes #56960

### Detail

This Pull Request changes the `activesupport/CHANGELOG.md` entry for Rails 8.0.3 to include a brief note explaining this overwrite precedence change. It adds a clear `Before` and `After` code example showing the behavior when a camelCase key and a snake_case key collide.

Opening this PR targeting the `8-0-stable` branch as requested in the issue.

combined_text:
Taste match with me on YouTube Music! Taste match with me on YouTube Music! https://music.youtube.com/playlist?list=RDTSJyMDIa3wrHrirJJ7o38Ngw&feature=share&jct=rNGaFFP_i5D6fbbi6sQUfQ Move object allocation out of loop  Right now BenchmarkCleaner allocates hundreds of strings on every request with an exception. This patch moves those strings to be generated at boot once and re-used.
## Bench Methods

I took a modified form of CodeTriage https://github.com/schneems/codetriage-ko1-test-app/blob/master/perf.rake and ran given rake tasks with and without the patch to BacktraceCleaner.

I made an endpoint results in exception

```
  def index
    raise “foo"
  end
```

The gem `memory_profiler` was used to capture objects allocated for a single request. Then `benchmark/ips` was used to test the speed of the patch.

You will see that we use less objects and the code becomes measurably faster with this patch.
## With patch:

Memory for one request:

```
Total allocated 7441
Total retained 37
```

Requests per second:

```
Calculating -------------------------------------
                 ips         4 i/100ms
-------------------------------------------------
                 ips       43.0 (±4.7%) i/s -        216 in   5.037733s
```
## Without patch:

Memory used for one request:

```
Total allocated 11599
Total retained 35 
```

Requests per second:

```
Calculating -------------------------------------
                 ips         3 i/100ms
-------------------------------------------------
                 ips       39.4 (±7.6%) i/s -        198 in   5.052783s
```
## Analysis

The patch is faster:

```
(43.0 - 39.4 ) / 39.4 * 100
# => 9 # % ~ speed bump
```

It also allocates less objects:

```
11599 - 7441
# => 4158
```

These strings are allocated on **EVERY SINGLE REQUEST**. This patch saves us 4158 objects **PER REQUEST** with exception.

Faster errors == Faster applications
